***Lost*** is an American [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction_on_television "Science fiction on television") [adventure](/wiki/Adventure_fiction "Adventure fiction") [drama](/wiki/Drama_(film_and_television) "Drama (film and television)") television series created by [Jeffrey Lieber](/wiki/Jeffrey_Lieber "Jeffrey Lieber"), [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams"), and [Damon Lindelof](/wiki/Damon_Lindelof "Damon Lindelof") that aired on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") from September 22, 2004, to May 23, 2010, with a total of [121 episodes](/wiki/List_of_Lost_episodes "List of Lost episodes") over six seasons. It contains elements of [supernatural fiction](/wiki/Supernatural_fiction "Supernatural fiction") and follows the survivors of a commercial [jet airliner](/wiki/Jet_airliner "Jet airliner") flying between [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") after the plane crashes on a mysterious [island](/wiki/Mythology_of_Lost#The_Island "Mythology of Lost") somewhere in the [South Pacific Ocean](/wiki/South_Pacific_Ocean "South Pacific Ocean"). Episodes typically feature a primary storyline set on the island, augmented by [flashback](/wiki/Flashback_(narrative) "Flashback (narrative)") or [flashforward](/wiki/Flashforward "Flashforward") sequences which provide additional insight into the involved characters.

Lindelof and [Carlton Cuse](/wiki/Carlton_Cuse "Carlton Cuse") served as [showrunners](/wiki/Showrunner "Showrunner") and were executive producers along with Abrams and [Bryan Burk](/wiki/Bryan_Burk "Bryan Burk"). Inspired by the 2000 film *[Cast Away](/wiki/Cast_Away "Cast Away")*,[[2]](#cite_note-Cast_Away-3) the show is told in a heavily serialized manner. Due to its large [ensemble cast](/wiki/Ensemble_cast "Ensemble cast") and the cost of filming primarily on location in [Oahu](/wiki/Oahu "Oahu"), Hawaii, the series was one of the most expensive on television, with the [pilot](/wiki/Pilot_(Lost) "Pilot (Lost)") alone costing over $14 million.[[3]](#cite_note-4) The [fictional universe](/wiki/Fictional_universe "Fictional universe") and [mythology of *Lost*](/wiki/Mythology_of_Lost "Mythology of Lost") were expanded upon by a number of related media—most importantly a series of mini-episodes, called *[Missing Pieces](/wiki/Lost:_Missing_Pieces "Lost: Missing Pieces")*, and a 12-minute [epilogue](/wiki/Epilogue "Epilogue") called "[The New Man in Charge](/wiki/The_New_Man_in_Charge "The New Man in Charge")".

*Lost* has regularly been ranked by critics as one of the [greatest television series of all time](/wiki/List_of_television_shows_considered_the_best "List of television shows considered the best").[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) The first season had an estimated average of 16 million viewers per episode on ABC.[[7]](#cite_note-8) During the [sixth and final season](/wiki/Lost_season_6 "Lost season 6"), the show averaged over 11 million U.S. viewers per episode. Nevertheless, the [series finale](/wiki/The_End_(Lost) "The End (Lost)") received a strongly polarized reaction from both fans and TV critics alike. *Lost* was the recipient of [hundreds of industry award nominations](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Lost "List of awards and nominations received by Lost") throughout its run and won numerous of these awards, including the [Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series") in 2005,[[8]](#cite_note-emmys-9) Best American Import at the [British Academy Television Awards](/wiki/British_Academy_Television_Awards "British Academy Television Awards") in 2005, the [Golden Globe Award for Best Television Series – Drama](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Television_Series_%E2%80%93_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Television Series – Drama") in 2006, and the [Screen Actors Guild Award for Outstanding Performance by an Ensemble in a Drama Series](/wiki/Screen_Actors_Guild_Award_for_Outstanding_Performance_by_an_Ensemble_in_a_Drama_Series "Screen Actors Guild Award for Outstanding Performance by an Ensemble in a Drama Series").

### Season 1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Season 1")]

Main article: [*Lost* season 1](/wiki/Lost_season_1 "Lost season 1")

Season 1 begins with the aftermath of a plane crash, which leaves the surviving passengers of Oceanic Airlines Flight 815 on what seems to be an uninhabited tropical island. Spinal surgeon [Jack Shephard](/wiki/Jack_Shephard "Jack Shephard") becomes their leader. Their survival is threatened by a number of mysterious entities, including [polar bears](/wiki/Polar_bear "Polar bear"), an unseen creature that roams the jungle (the "[Smoke Monster](/wiki/Man_in_Black_(Lost) "Man in Black (Lost)")"), and the island's malevolent inhabitants, known as ["The Others"](/wiki/List_of_Lost_characters#The_Others "List of Lost characters"). They encounter a French woman named [Danielle Rousseau](/wiki/Danielle_Rousseau "Danielle Rousseau"), who was shipwrecked on the island 16 years before the crash and is desperate for news of her daughter, [Alex](/wiki/Alex_Rousseau "Alex Rousseau"). They also find a mysterious metal hatch buried in the ground. While two survivors, [Locke](/wiki/John_Locke_(Lost) "John Locke (Lost)") and [Boone](/wiki/Boone_Carlyle "Boone Carlyle"), try to open the hatch, [Michael](/wiki/Michael_Dawson_(Lost) "Michael Dawson (Lost)"), [Jin](/wiki/Jin-Soo_Kwon "Jin-Soo Kwon"), [Walt](/wiki/Walt_Lloyd "Walt Lloyd"), and [Sawyer](/wiki/James_%22Sawyer%22_Ford "James \"Sawyer\" Ford") attempt to leave on a raft that they have built. Meanwhile, [flashbacks](/wiki/Flashback_(narrative) "Flashback (narrative)") center on details of the individual survivors' lives prior to the plane crash.

### Season 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Season 2")]

Main article: [*Lost* season 2](/wiki/Lost_season_2 "Lost season 2")

Season 2 follows the growing conflict between the survivors and the Others. The four survivors on the raft are ambushed by the Others, and they take Michael's son Walt. The survivors are forced to return to the island, where they find the tail-section survivors (the "Tailies"). A power struggle between Jack and [Locke](/wiki/John_Locke_(Lost) "John Locke (Lost)") over control of the guns and medicine located in the hatch develops and is resolved by Sawyer when he gains control of them. The hatch is revealed to be a research station built 30 years earlier by the [DHARMA Initiative](/wiki/Dharma_Initiative "Dharma Initiative"), a scientific research project that involved conducting experiments on the island. A man named [Desmond Hume](/wiki/Desmond_Hume "Desmond Hume") had been living in the hatch for three years, activating a computer program every 108 minutes to prevent an unknown catastrophic event from occurring. To recover his son, Michael betrays the survivors, and Jack, Sawyer, and [Kate](/wiki/Kate_Austen "Kate Austen") are captured. Michael is given a boat and leaves the island with his son, while Locke destroys the computer in the hatch, resulting in an electromagnetic discharge. This causes the island to be detected by scientists working for [Penny Widmore](/wiki/Penny_Widmore "Penny Widmore"), and it is revealed that a similar event caused the breakup of the plane.

### Season 3

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Season 3")]

Main article: [*Lost* season 3](/wiki/Lost_season_3 "Lost season 3")

In season 3, the crash survivors learn more about the Others and their long history on the mysterious island, along with the fate of the Dharma Initiative. The leader of the Others, [Ben Linus](/wiki/Ben_Linus "Ben Linus"), is introduced and defections from both sides pave the way for conflict between the two. [Time travel](/wiki/Time_travel "Time travel") elements also begin to appear in the series, as Desmond is forced to turn the fail-safe key in the hatch to stop the electromagnetic event, and this sends his mind eight years in the past. When he returns to the present, he is able to see the future. Kate and Sawyer escape the Others, while Jack stays after Ben promises that Jack will be able to leave the island in a submarine if he operates on Ben, who has cancer. Jack does, but the submarine is destroyed by Locke. Jack is left behind with [Juliet](/wiki/Juliet_Burke "Juliet Burke"), an Other, who also seeks to leave the island, while Locke joins the Others. A helicopter carrying [Naomi](/wiki/Naomi_Dorrit "Naomi Dorrit") crashes near the island. Naomi says her freighter, *Kahana*, is eighty miles offshore and was sent by Penny, Desmond's ex-girlfriend. Desmond has a vision in which [Charlie](/wiki/Charlie_Pace "Charlie Pace") will drown after shutting down a signal that prevents communication with the outside world. His vision comes true, but Charlie speaks with Penny, who says she does not know Naomi. Before drowning, Charlie writes "Not Penny's boat" on his hand so that Desmond can warn the other survivors. Meanwhile, the survivors make contact with a rescue team aboard the freighter. In the [season finale](/wiki/Through_the_Looking_Glass_(Lost) "Through the Looking Glass (Lost)"), apparent flashbacks show a depressed Jack going to an unknown person's funeral. In the final scene, these are revealed to be "flashforwards", and Kate and Jack are revealed to have escaped the island, but Jack believes that they "have to go back".

### Season 4

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Season 4")]

Main article: [*Lost* season 4](/wiki/Lost_season_4 "Lost season 4")

Season 4 focuses on the survivors dealing with the arrival of people from the freighter, who have been sent to the island to reclaim it from Ben. "Flashforwards" continue, in which it is seen how six survivors, dubbed the "Oceanic Six", live their lives after escaping the island. The "Oceanic Six" are Jack, Kate, [Hurley](/wiki/Hugo_%22Hurley%22_Reyes "Hugo \"Hurley\" Reyes"), [Sayid](/wiki/Sayid_Jarrah "Sayid Jarrah"), [Sun](/wiki/Sun-Hwa_Kwon "Sun-Hwa Kwon"), and Aaron. In the present, four members of the freighter arrive and team up with the survivors to escape the island, since the crew of the freighter have orders to kill everyone who stays. Meanwhile, Ben travels with Locke to see [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob_(Lost) "Jacob (Lost)"), the island's leader. Locke enters his cabin but finds Jack's dead father, [Christian](/wiki/Christian_Shephard "Christian Shephard"), who says he can speak on Jacob's behalf, and orders Locke to "move" the island. Ben takes Locke to an underground station in which time travel was researched. Locke becomes the new leader of the Others, while Ben moves the island by turning a frozen wheel, after which he is transported to the [Sahara](/wiki/Sahara "Sahara"). The six survivors escape in a helicopter as they watch the island disappear and are subsequently rescued by Penny. It is then that Desmond and Penny reunite. In the season's finale, it is revealed that the funeral Jack went to in the "flashforward" was that of Locke, who had been seeking out the Oceanic Six in his efforts to convince them to return to the island.

### Season 5

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Season 5")]

Main article: [*Lost* season 5](/wiki/Lost_season_5 "Lost season 5")

Season 5 follows two timelines. The first timeline takes place on the island where the survivors who were left behind erratically jump forward and backward through time. In one of these time periods, Locke speaks with [Richard Alpert](/wiki/Richard_Alpert_(Lost) "Richard Alpert (Lost)"), one of the Others, who says that to save the island, he must bring everyone back. Locke goes to the same underground station Ben went to. After moving the island himself, Locke is transported to the Sahara in 2007, as the time shifts on the island stop and the survivors are stranded with the DHARMA Initiative in 1974. In 2007, Locke contacts the Oceanic Six but fails to convince them to return. Locke tells Jack his father is alive on the island. This causes Jack to start taking flights every week, hoping to crash back on the island. Ben finds Locke and kills him. After Locke's death, the Oceanic Six are told to board Ajira Airways Flight 316 to return to the island and in order to go back, they have to take Locke's body in the plane. They take the flight, but some land in 1977, where they meet with the other survivors who are now part of the DHARMA Initiative, and others land in 2007. The survivors in 1977 are told by [Daniel Faraday](/wiki/Daniel_Faraday "Daniel Faraday") that if they detonate a hydrogen bomb at the Swan construction site, the electromagnetic energy below it will be destroyed; as a result, the hatch would never be built, and the plane would not crash. In 2007, Locke apparently comes back to life. He instructs Richard to speak with a time-traveling Locke and tell him that he must bring everyone back to the island. After this, he goes to speak with Jacob. The season finale reveals that Locke is still dead and another entity has taken over his form to manipulate Ben into killing Jacob. In 1977, Juliet detonates the fission core taken from the hydrogen bomb.

### Season 6

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Season 6")]

Main article: [*Lost* season 6](/wiki/Lost_season_6 "Lost season 6")

Season 6, the final season, also follows two timelines. In the first timeline, the survivors are sent to the present day, as the death of Jacob allows his brother, the [Man in Black](/wiki/Man_in_Black_(Lost) "Man in Black (Lost)") (the original form of the Smoke Monster) to take over the island. Having assumed the form of Locke, the Smoke Monster seeks to escape the island and forces a final war between the forces of good and evil.

The second timeline, called the "flash-sideways" narrative, follows the lives of the main characters in a setting where Oceanic 815 never crashed. In the final episodes, a [flashback to the distant past](/wiki/Across_the_Sea_(Lost) "Across the Sea (Lost)") shows the origins of the island's power and of the conflict between Jacob and the Man in Black, who are revealed to be twin brothers, with Jacob desperate to keep his brother from leaving the island after he is [transmogrified](/wiki/Transmogrified "Transmogrified") by the power of the island and becomes the Smoke Monster.

Jacob's machinations are revealed: everyone was pushed by fate and his manipulation to be on the Oceanic flight as many of the members of the flight were deemed "candidates" by Jacob to be the new protector of the island after his death. The Man in Black's mission since the beginning was to kill all of the candidates, thereby allowing him to leave the island once and for all. The ghost of Jacob appears to the surviving candidates, and Jack is appointed as the new protector. Jack catches up with the Man in Black, who says that he wants to go to the "Heart of the Island" to turn it off and finally leave the island. They reach the place, but after doing this, the Man in Black becomes mortal. The Man in Black is killed by Jack and Kate, but Jack is seriously injured in the process. Hurley becomes the new protector of the island. Several of the survivors die in the conflict or stay on the island, and the remaining escape in the Ajira plane once and for all. Jack returns to the "Heart of the Island" and turns it on again, saving it. Hurley, as the new protector, asks Ben to help him in his new job, which he agrees to do. Having saved the island, Jack dies peacefully in the same place in which he woke up when he arrived on the island.

The [series finale](/wiki/The_End_(Lost) "The End (Lost)") reveals that the flash-sideways timeline is actually a form of [purgatory](/wiki/Purgatory "Purgatory") in the afterlife, where some of the survivors and other characters from the island are reunited after death. In the last scene, the survivors are all reunited in a church where they "move on" together.

### Mythology and interpretations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Mythology and interpretations")]

Main article: [Mythology of *Lost*](/wiki/Mythology_of_Lost "Mythology of Lost")

Episodes of *Lost* include a number of mysterious elements ascribed to science fiction or supernatural phenomena. The creators of the series refer to these elements as composing the [mythology](/wiki/Canon_(fiction) "Canon (fiction)") of the series, and they formed the basis of fan speculation.[[15]](#cite_note-16) The show's mythological elements include a "[Smoke Monster](/wiki/Mythology_of_Lost#The_Man_in_Black/Smoke_Monster "Mythology of Lost")" that roams the island, a mysterious group of inhabitants whom the survivors called "[The Others](/wiki/Others_(Lost) "Others (Lost)")" or "Natives", a scientific organization called the [Dharma Initiative](/wiki/Dharma_Initiative "Dharma Initiative") that placed several research stations on the island, a [sequence of numbers](/wiki/The_Lost_numbers "The Lost numbers") that frequently appears in the lives of the characters in the past, present, and future, and personal connections ([synchronicity](/wiki/Synchronicity "Synchronicity")) between the characters of which they are often unaware.

At the heart of the series is a complex and cryptic storyline, which spawned numerous questions and discussions among viewers.[[16]](#cite_note-ign_loose_ends-17) Encouraged by *Lost*'s writers and stars, who often interacted with fans online, viewers and TV critics alike took to widespread theorizing in an attempt to unravel the mysteries. Theories mainly concerned the nature of the island, the origins of the "Monster" and the "Others", the meaning of the numbers, and the reasons for both the crash and the survival of some passengers.[[16]](#cite_note-ign_loose_ends-17) Several of the more common fan theories were discussed and rejected by the show's creators, the most common being that the survivors of Oceanic flight 815 are dead and in [purgatory](/wiki/Purgatory "Purgatory"). Lindelof rejected speculation that [spaceships](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft") or [aliens](/wiki/Extraterrestrial_life_in_popular_culture "Extraterrestrial life in popular culture") influenced the events on the island or that everything seen was a fictional reality taking place in someone's mind. Carlton Cuse dismissed the theory that the island was a reality TV show and the castaways unwitting housemates,[[17]](#cite_note-18) and Lindelof many times denied the theory that the "Monster" was a [nanobot](/wiki/Nanobot "Nanobot") cloud similar to the one featured in [Michael Crichton](/wiki/Michael_Crichton "Michael Crichton")'s novel *[Prey](/wiki/Prey_(novel) "Prey (novel)")* (which happened to share the protagonist's name, Jack).[[18]](#cite_note-19)

#### Recurring elements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Recurring elements")]

There are several recurring elements and [motifs](/wiki/Motif_(literature) "Motif (literature)") on *Lost*, which generally have no direct effect on the story itself but expand the show's literary and philosophical [subtext](/wiki/Subtext "Subtext"). These elements include frequent appearances of black and white, which reflect the dualism within characters and situations; as well as rebellion in almost all characters, especially Kate;[[19]](#cite_note-20) [dysfunctional family](/wiki/Dysfunctional_family "Dysfunctional family") situations (especially ones that revolve around the fathers of many characters), as portrayed in the lives of nearly all the main characters;[[20]](#cite_note-21) apocalyptic references, including Desmond's pushing the button to forestall the end of the world; coincidence versus fate, revealed most apparently through the juxtaposition of the characters Locke and Mr. Eko; conflict between science and faith, embodied by the leadership tug-of-war between Jack and Locke and their stark disagreements on subjects such as the hatch, the button, and leaving the island;[[21]](#cite_note-22) the struggle between good and evil, shown by the relationship between Jacob and the Man in Black, several times by Locke using symbols such as his [backgammon](/wiki/Backgammon "Backgammon") set, also the white and black rocks that the [Man in Black](/wiki/Man_in_Black_(Lost) "Man in Black (Lost)") referred to as an "inside joke"; and references to numerous works of literature, including mentions and discussions of particular novels.[[22]](#cite_note-USAToday10042005-23) One notable reference to a novel is [John Steinbeck](/wiki/John_Steinbeck "John Steinbeck")'s *[Of Mice and Men](/wiki/Of_Mice_and_Men "Of Mice and Men")*, usually when Sawyer is seen reading it or referencing it. There are also many allusions in characters' names to famous historical thinkers and writers, such as [Ben Linus](/wiki/Ben_Linus "Ben Linus") (after chemist [Linus Pauling](/wiki/Linus_Pauling "Linus Pauling")), John Locke (after the [philosopher](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke")) and his alias [Jeremy Bentham](/wiki/Jeremy_Bentham_(Lost) "Jeremy Bentham (Lost)") (after the [philosopher](/wiki/Jeremy_Bentham "Jeremy Bentham")), [Danielle Rousseau](/wiki/Danielle_Rousseau "Danielle Rousseau") (after philosopher [Jean-Jacques Rousseau](/wiki/Jean-Jacques_Rousseau "Jean-Jacques Rousseau")), [Desmond David Hume](/wiki/Desmond_Hume "Desmond Hume") (after philosopher [David Hume](/wiki/David_Hume "David Hume")), Juliet's ex-husband (after philosopher [Edmund Burke](/wiki/Edmund_Burke "Edmund Burke")), [Mikhail Bakunin](/wiki/Mikhail_Bakunin_(Lost) "Mikhail Bakunin (Lost)") (after the [anarchist philosopher](/wiki/Mikhail_Bakunin "Mikhail Bakunin")), Daniel Faraday (after physicist [Michael Faraday](/wiki/Michael_Faraday "Michael Faraday")), [Eloise Hawking](/wiki/Characters_of_Lost#Miscellaneous_characters "Characters of Lost") (after physicist [Stephen Hawking](/wiki/Stephen_Hawking "Stephen Hawking")), George Minkowski (after mathematician [Hermann Minkowski](/wiki/Hermann_Minkowski "Hermann Minkowski")), [Richard Alpert](/wiki/Richard_Alpert_(Lost) "Richard Alpert (Lost)") (the birth name of spiritual teacher [Ram Dass](/wiki/Ram_Dass "Ram Dass")), Boone Carlyle (after pioneer [Daniel Boone](/wiki/Daniel_Boone "Daniel Boone") and philosopher [Thomas Carlyle](/wiki/Thomas_Carlyle "Thomas Carlyle")), and [Charlotte Staples Lewis](/wiki/Charlotte_Lewis_(Lost) "Charlotte Lewis (Lost)") (after author [Clive Staples (C.S.) Lewis](/wiki/C._S._Lewis "C. S. Lewis")).[[23]](#cite_note-24)

## Cast and characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Cast and characters")]

Main article: [List of *Lost* characters](/wiki/List_of_Lost_characters "List of Lost characters")

[![The main actors from Lost, standing side by side.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/9/91/Main_characters_of_Lost.jpg)](/wiki/File:Main_characters_of_Lost.jpg)

From left to right: [Faraday](/wiki/Daniel_Faraday "Daniel Faraday"), [Boone](/wiki/Boone_Carlyle "Boone Carlyle"), [Miles](/wiki/Miles_Straume "Miles Straume"), [Michael](/wiki/Michael_Dawson_(Lost) "Michael Dawson (Lost)"), [Ana Lucia](/wiki/Ana_Lucia_Cortez "Ana Lucia Cortez"), [Charlotte](/wiki/Charlotte_Lewis_(Lost) "Charlotte Lewis (Lost)"), [Frank](/wiki/Frank_Lapidus "Frank Lapidus"), [Shannon](/wiki/Shannon_Rutherford "Shannon Rutherford"), [Desmond](/wiki/Desmond_Hume "Desmond Hume"), [Eko](/wiki/Mr._Eko "Mr. Eko"), [Kate](/wiki/Kate_Austen "Kate Austen"), [Jack](/wiki/Jack_Shephard "Jack Shephard"), [Sawyer](/wiki/James_%22Sawyer%22_Ford "James \"Sawyer\" Ford"), [Locke](/wiki/John_Locke_(Lost) "John Locke (Lost)"), [Ben](/wiki/Ben_Linus "Ben Linus"), [Sayid](/wiki/Sayid_Jarrah "Sayid Jarrah"), [Libby](/wiki/Libby_(Lost) "Libby (Lost)"), [Sun](/wiki/Sun-Hwa_Kwon "Sun-Hwa Kwon"), [Jin](/wiki/Jin-Soo_Kwon "Jin-Soo Kwon"), [Claire](/wiki/Claire_Littleton "Claire Littleton"), [Hurley](/wiki/Hugo_%22Hurley%22_Reyes "Hugo \"Hurley\" Reyes"), [Juliet](/wiki/Juliet_Burke "Juliet Burke"), [Charlie](/wiki/Charlie_Pace "Charlie Pace"), [Richard](/wiki/Richard_Alpert_(Lost) "Richard Alpert (Lost)"), [Bernard](/wiki/Rose_and_Bernard_Nadler "Rose and Bernard Nadler"), [Rose](/wiki/Rose_and_Bernard_Nadler "Rose and Bernard Nadler"), and [Vincent](/wiki/Characters_of_Lost#Vincent "Characters of Lost")

Of the 324 people on board [Oceanic Flight 815](/wiki/Oceanic_Flight_815 "Oceanic Flight 815"),[[24]](#cite_note-25) there are 70 initial survivors (as well as one dog) spread across the three sections of the plane crash.[[25]](#cite_note-Pilot:_Part_2-26)[[26]](#cite_note-White_Rabbit-27)[[27]](#cite_note-The_Other_48_Days-28) Although a large cast made *Lost* more expensive to produce, the writers benefited from added flexibility in story decisions.[[28]](#cite_note-large_cast-29) According to series executive producer Bryan Burk, "You can have more interactions between characters and create more diverse characters, more back stories, more [love triangles](/wiki/Love_triangle "Love triangle")."[[28]](#cite_note-large_cast-29)

*Lost* was planned as a multicultural show with an international cast. The initial season had 14 regular speaking roles that received star [billing](/wiki/Billing_(film) "Billing (film)"); though most of these actors remained on the show for the duration of its run (including flashbacks and alternate timelines), no single actor appeared in all 121 episodes. [Matthew Fox](/wiki/Matthew_Fox "Matthew Fox") played the protagonist, a troubled surgeon named [Jack Shephard](/wiki/Jack_Shephard "Jack Shephard"). [Evangeline Lilly](/wiki/Evangeline_Lilly "Evangeline Lilly") portrayed fugitive [Kate Austen](/wiki/Kate_Austen "Kate Austen"). [Jorge Garcia](/wiki/Jorge_Garcia "Jorge Garcia") played [Hugo "Hurley" Reyes](/wiki/Hugo_%22Hurley%22_Reyes "Hugo \"Hurley\" Reyes"), an unlucky lottery winner. [Josh Holloway](/wiki/Josh_Holloway "Josh Holloway") played a con man, [James "Sawyer" Ford](/wiki/James_%22Sawyer%22_Ford "James \"Sawyer\" Ford"). [Ian Somerhalder](/wiki/Ian_Somerhalder "Ian Somerhalder") played [Boone Carlyle](/wiki/Boone_Carlyle "Boone Carlyle"), chief operating officer of his mother's wedding business, and [Maggie Grace](/wiki/Maggie_Grace "Maggie Grace") played his stepsister [Shannon Rutherford](/wiki/Shannon_Rutherford "Shannon Rutherford"), a former dance teacher. [Harold Perrineau](/wiki/Harold_Perrineau "Harold Perrineau") portrayed construction worker and aspiring artist [Michael Dawson](/wiki/Michael_Dawson_(Lost) "Michael Dawson (Lost)"), while [Malcolm David Kelley](/wiki/Malcolm_David_Kelley "Malcolm David Kelley") played his young son, [Walt Lloyd](/wiki/Walt_Lloyd "Walt Lloyd"). [Terry O'Quinn](/wiki/Terry_O%27Quinn "Terry O'Quinn") played the mysterious [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke_(Lost) "John Locke (Lost)"). [Naveen Andrews](/wiki/Naveen_Andrews "Naveen Andrews") portrayed former [Iraqi Republican Guard](/wiki/Iraqi_Republican_Guard "Iraqi Republican Guard") [Sayid Jarrah](/wiki/Sayid_Jarrah "Sayid Jarrah"). [Emilie de Ravin](/wiki/Emilie_de_Ravin "Emilie de Ravin") played a young Australian mother-to-be, [Claire Littleton](/wiki/Claire_Littleton "Claire Littleton"). [Yunjin Kim](/wiki/Yunjin_Kim "Yunjin Kim") played [Sun-Hwa Kwon](/wiki/Sun-Hwa_Kwon "Sun-Hwa Kwon"), the daughter of a powerful and incredibly wealthy Korean businessman and mobster, with [Daniel Dae Kim](/wiki/Daniel_Dae_Kim "Daniel Dae Kim") as her husband and father's enforcer [Jin-Soo Kwon](/wiki/Jin-Soo_Kwon "Jin-Soo Kwon"). [Dominic Monaghan](/wiki/Dominic_Monaghan "Dominic Monaghan") played English ex-rock star and drug addict [Charlie Pace](/wiki/Charlie_Pace "Charlie Pace").

### Seasons 1 and 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Seasons 1 and 2")]

During the first two seasons, some characters were written out, while new characters with new stories were added.[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31) Boone Carlyle was written out near the end of season one,[[31]](#cite_note-boonedeathdev-32) and Walt became an intermittent character, making occasional appearances throughout season two after he is captured by The Others in the season one finale. Shannon's departure eight episodes into season two made way for newcomers [Mr. Eko](/wiki/Mr._Eko "Mr. Eko"), a former Nigerian militia leader and fake Catholic priest played by [Adewale Akinnuoye-Agbaje](/wiki/Adewale_Akinnuoye-Agbaje "Adewale Akinnuoye-Agbaje"); [Ana Lucia Cortez](/wiki/Ana_Lucia_Cortez "Ana Lucia Cortez"), an airport [TSA](/wiki/Transportation_Security_Administration "Transportation Security Administration") guard and former [LAPD](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Police_Department "Los Angeles Police Department") police officer played by [Michelle Rodriguez](/wiki/Michelle_Rodriguez "Michelle Rodriguez"); and [Libby Smith](/wiki/Libby_(Lost) "Libby (Lost)"), a purported clinical psychologist and formerly mentally ill woman portrayed by [Cynthia Watros](/wiki/Cynthia_Watros "Cynthia Watros"). Ana Lucia and Libby were written out of the series toward the end of season two after being shot by Michael, who then left the island along with his son.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

### Seasons 3 and 4

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Seasons 3 and 4")]

In season three, two actors were promoted from recurring to starring roles: [Henry Ian Cusick](/wiki/Henry_Ian_Cusick "Henry Ian Cusick") as former Scottish soldier [Desmond Hume](/wiki/Desmond_Hume "Desmond Hume"); and [Michael Emerson](/wiki/Michael_Emerson "Michael Emerson") as the manipulative leader of the Others, [Ben Linus](/wiki/Ben_Linus "Ben Linus"). In addition, three new actors joined the regular cast: [Elizabeth Mitchell](/wiki/Elizabeth_Mitchell "Elizabeth Mitchell"), as fertility doctor and Other [Juliet Burke](/wiki/Juliet_Burke "Juliet Burke"); and [Kiele Sanchez](/wiki/Kiele_Sanchez "Kiele Sanchez") and [Rodrigo Santoro](/wiki/Rodrigo_Santoro "Rodrigo Santoro") as background survivor couple [Nikki Fernandez](/wiki/Nikki_and_Paulo "Nikki and Paulo") and [Paulo](/wiki/Nikki_and_Paulo "Nikki and Paulo"). Several characters died in the season: Eko was killed off when Akinnuoye-Agbaje did not wish to continue on the show,[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35) Nikki and Paulo were buried alive mid-season after poor fan response,[[35]](#cite_note-36) and in the third-season finale, Charlie dies a hero.

In season four, Harold Perrineau rejoined the main cast to reprise the role of Michael, now [suicidal](/wiki/Suicidal_ideation "Suicidal ideation") and on a desperate redemptive journey to atone for his previous crimes.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Along with Perrineau, additional new actors—[Jeremy Davies](/wiki/Jeremy_Davies "Jeremy Davies") as [Daniel Faraday](/wiki/Daniel_Faraday "Daniel Faraday"), a nervous physicist who takes a scientific interest in the island; [Ken Leung](/wiki/Ken_Leung "Ken Leung") as [Miles Straume](/wiki/Miles_Straume "Miles Straume"), a sarcastic supposed ghost whisperer; and [Rebecca Mader](/wiki/Rebecca_Mader "Rebecca Mader") as [Charlotte Staples Lewis](/wiki/Charlotte_Staples_Lewis "Charlotte Staples Lewis"), a hard-headed and determined anthropologist and successful academic—joined the cast.[[37]](#cite_note-38) Michael was written out in the fourth-season finale.[[38]](#cite_note-39) Claire, who mysteriously disappears with her dead father near the end of the season, did not return as a series regular for the fifth season but returned for the sixth and final season.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

### Seasons 5 and 6

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Seasons 5 and 6")]

In season five, no new characters joined the main cast; however, several characters exited the show: Charlotte was written out early in the season in episode five, with Daniel being written out later in the third to last episode. Season six saw several cast changes: Juliet was written out in the season premiere while three previous recurring characters were upgraded to starring status.[[40]](#cite_note-Nov19PR-41) These included [Néstor Carbonell](/wiki/N%C3%A9stor_Carbonell "Néstor Carbonell") as mysterious, age-less Other [Richard Alpert](/wiki/Richard_Alpert_(Lost) "Richard Alpert (Lost)"); [Jeff Fahey](/wiki/Jeff_Fahey "Jeff Fahey") as pilot [Frank Lapidus](/wiki/Frank_Lapidus "Frank Lapidus");[[41]](#cite_note-lostqausatoday-42) and [Zuleikha Robinson](/wiki/Zuleikha_Robinson "Zuleikha Robinson") as Ajira Airways Flight 316 survivor [Ilana Verdansky](/wiki/Ilana_Verdansky "Ilana Verdansky"). Additionally, former cast members Ian Somerhalder, Dominic Monaghan, Rebecca Mader, Jeremy Davies, Elizabeth Mitchell, Maggie Grace,[[42]](#cite_note-43) Michelle Rodriguez,[[43]](#cite_note-44) Harold Perrineau, and Cynthia Watros[[44]](#cite_note-45) made return appearances.

### Supporting characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Supporting characters")]

Numerous supporting characters were given expansive and recurring appearances in the progressive storyline. [Danielle Rousseau](/wiki/Danielle_Rousseau "Danielle Rousseau") ([Mira Furlan](/wiki/Mira_Furlan "Mira Furlan"))—a French member of an earlier scientific expedition to the island, first encountered as a voice recording in the pilot episode—appears throughout the series. She is searching for her daughter, who later turns up in the form of [Alex Rousseau](/wiki/Alex_(Lost) "Alex (Lost)") ([Tania Raymonde](/wiki/Tania_Raymonde "Tania Raymonde")). Alex has been kidnapped by Ben Linus and brought to the Others where she was raised. [Cindy](/wiki/Cindy_Chandler_(Lost) "Cindy Chandler (Lost)") ([Kimberley Joseph](/wiki/Kimberley_Joseph "Kimberley Joseph")), an Oceanic flight attendant who first appeared in the pilot, survived the crash and, subsequently, became one of the Others.

In the second season, married couple [Rose Nadler](/wiki/Rose_and_Bernard_Nadler "Rose and Bernard Nadler") ([L. Scott Caldwell](/wiki/L._Scott_Caldwell "L. Scott Caldwell")) and [Bernard Nadler](/wiki/Bernard_Nadler "Bernard Nadler") ([Sam Anderson](/wiki/Sam_Anderson "Sam Anderson")), separated on opposite sides of the island (she with the main characters, he with the tail section survivors), were featured in a flashback episode after being reunited. The second season also introduces Dr. Pierre Chang ([Francois Chau](/wiki/Francois_Chau "Francois Chau")), a member of the mysterious [Dharma Initiative](/wiki/Dharma_Initiative "Dharma Initiative") who appears in the orientation films for its numerous stations located throughout the island. Corporate magnate [Charles Widmore](/wiki/Charles_Widmore "Charles Widmore") ([Alan Dale](/wiki/Alan_Dale "Alan Dale")) has connections to both Ben and Desmond. Desmond is in love with Widmore's daughter [Penelope "Penny" Widmore](/wiki/Characters_of_Lost#Penelope_"Penny"_Widmore "Characters of Lost") ([Sonya Walger](/wiki/Sonya_Walger "Sonya Walger")).

Eloise Hawking ([Fionnula Flanagan](/wiki/Fionnula_Flanagan "Fionnula Flanagan")), introduced in the third season, is Daniel Faraday's mother and also has connections with Desmond. The introduction of the Others featured [Tom Friendly](/wiki/Tom_Friendly "Tom Friendly") ([M. C. Gainey](/wiki/M._C._Gainey "M. C. Gainey")), and [Ethan Rom](/wiki/Ethan_Rom "Ethan Rom") ([William Mapother](/wiki/William_Mapother "William Mapother")), all of whom have been shown in both flashbacks and the ongoing story. Jack's father [Christian Shephard](/wiki/Christian_Shephard "Christian Shephard") ([John Terry](/wiki/John_Terry_(actor) "John Terry (actor)")) has appeared in multiple flashbacks of various characters. In the third season, [Naomi Dorrit](/wiki/Characters_of_Lost#Naomi_Dorrit "Characters of Lost") ([Marsha Thomason](/wiki/Marsha_Thomason "Marsha Thomason")), the team leader of a group hired by Widmore to find Ben Linus, parachutes onto the island. One member of her team includes the ruthless mercenary [Martin Keamy](/wiki/Martin_Keamy "Martin Keamy") ([Kevin Durand](/wiki/Kevin_Durand "Kevin Durand")).

In the finale episode "[The End](/wiki/The_End_(Lost) "The End (Lost)")", recurring guest stars [Sam Anderson](/wiki/Sam_Anderson "Sam Anderson"); [L. Scott Caldwell](/wiki/L._Scott_Caldwell "L. Scott Caldwell"); [Francois Chau](/wiki/Francois_Chau "Francois Chau"); [Fionnula Flanagan](/wiki/Fionnula_Flanagan "Fionnula Flanagan"); [Sonya Walger](/wiki/Sonya_Walger "Sonya Walger"); and [John Terry](/wiki/John_Terry_(actor) "John Terry (actor)") were credited under the "starring" rubric alongside the principal cast. The mysterious black smoke cloud-like entity, known as "[the Monster](/wiki/Man_in_Black_(Lost) "Man in Black (Lost)")", appeared in human form during season five and six as a middle-aged man dressed in black robes, who was played by [Titus Welliver](/wiki/Titus_Welliver "Titus Welliver"), and in season six, it appears in the form of John Locke played by O'Quinn in a [dual role](/wiki/Dual_role "Dual role"). His rival, [Jacob](/wiki/Jacob_(Lost) "Jacob (Lost)"), was played by [Mark Pellegrino](/wiki/Mark_Pellegrino "Mark Pellegrino").

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Production")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Lost_%28ABC_television_series%29_Bad_Robot_logo.svg/250px-Lost_%28ABC_television_series%29_Bad_Robot_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Lost_(ABC_television_series)_Bad_Robot_logo.svg)

Official production logo, initially used before the airing of the series.

[![Damon Lindelof and Carlton Cuse sitting, speaking into microphones.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Lindelof_y_Cuse.jpg/250px-Lindelof_y_Cuse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lindelof_y_Cuse.jpg)

Damon Lindelof (*left*) co-created the series and served as an executive producer and showrunner alongside Carlton Cuse (*right*).

*Lost* was produced by [ABC Studios](/wiki/ABC_Studios "ABC Studios"), [Bad Robot](/wiki/Bad_Robot "Bad Robot"), and Grass Skirt Productions. Throughout its run, the executive producers of the series were [Damon Lindelof](/wiki/Damon_Lindelof "Damon Lindelof"), [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams"), [Bryan Burk](/wiki/Bryan_Burk "Bryan Burk"), [Carlton Cuse](/wiki/Carlton_Cuse "Carlton Cuse"), [Jack Bender](/wiki/Jack_Bender "Jack Bender"), [Jeff Pinkner](/wiki/Jeff_Pinkner "Jeff Pinkner"), [Edward Kitsis](/wiki/Edward_Kitsis "Edward Kitsis"), [Adam Horowitz](/wiki/Adam_Horowitz_(screenwriter) "Adam Horowitz (screenwriter)"), [Jean Higgins](/wiki/Jean_Higgins "Jean Higgins"), and [Elizabeth Sarnoff](/wiki/Elizabeth_Sarnoff "Elizabeth Sarnoff"), with Lindelof and Cuse serving as [showrunners](/wiki/Showrunner "Showrunner").[[40]](#cite_note-Nov19PR-41)

### Conception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Conception")]

The series was conceived by [Lloyd Braun](/wiki/Lloyd_Braun "Lloyd Braun"), head of ABC at the time, while he was on vacation in Hawaii during 2003, as a television adaptation of the 2000 [Tom Hanks](/wiki/Tom_Hanks "Tom Hanks") film *Cast Away*, crossed with elements of the popular reality show *[Survivor](/wiki/Survivor_(American_TV_series) "Survivor (American TV series)")*.[[45]](#cite_note-EW-46) Braun later pitched his ideas for *Cast Away – The Series* at the network's gathering of executives at the [Disney's Grand Californian Hotel & Spa](/wiki/Disney%27s_Grand_Californian_Hotel_%26_Spa "Disney's Grand Californian Hotel & Spa") in [Anaheim, California](/wiki/Anaheim,_California "Anaheim, California"), describing the concept as "parts *Cast Away*, *Survivor*, and *[Gilligan's Island](/wiki/Gilligan%27s_Island "Gilligan's Island")*, with a *[Lord of the Flies](/wiki/Lord_of_the_Flies "Lord of the Flies")* element."[[46]](#cite_note-47) Many found the idea laughable, but senior vice president Thom Sherman saw potential and decided to order an initial script from [Spelling Television](/wiki/Spelling_Television "Spelling Television"). Spelling producer Ted Gold turned to writer [Jeffrey Lieber](/wiki/Jeffrey_Lieber "Jeffrey Lieber"), who presented a pitch to ABC in September 2003 titled *Nowhere*, which Sherman approved. Unhappy with the eventual script by Lieber and a subsequent rewrite, in January 2004, Braun contacted J. J. Abrams, who had developed the TV series *[Alias](/wiki/Alias_(TV_series) "Alias (TV series)")* for ABC, to write a new pilot script. Lieber would later receive a story credit for the *Lost* pilot and, subsequently, shared the "created by" credit with Abrams and Lindelof, after a request for [arbitration](/wiki/WGA_screenwriting_credit_system "WGA screenwriting credit system") at the [Writers Guild of America](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America,_East "Writers Guild of America, East").[[2]](#cite_note-Cast_Away-3) The one inviolable edict Braun made to Abrams was that the show's title must be *Lost*, having conceived of the title and being angry at its change to *Nowhere* by Lieber.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

Although initially hesitant, Abrams warmed to the idea on the condition that the series would have a [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural") angle to it and if he had a writing partner.[[45]](#cite_note-EW-46)[[48]](#cite_note-DT081405-49) ABC executive Heather Kadin sent him [Damon Lindelof](/wiki/Damon_Lindelof "Damon Lindelof"), who had long intended to meet Abrams as he wished to write for *Alias*.[[49]](#cite_note-50) Together, Abrams and Lindelof created the series' style and characters and also wrote a [series bible](/wiki/Bible_(writing) "Bible (writing)") that conceived and detailed the major [mythological](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology") ideas and plot points for an ideal four-to-five-season run for the show.[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52) The novel idea of a story arc spanning several years was inspired by *[Babylon 5](/wiki/Babylon_5 "Babylon 5").*[[52]](#cite_note-53) Because ABC felt that *Alias* was too [serialized](/wiki/Serial_drama "Serial drama"), Lindelof and Abrams assured the network in the bible that the show would be self-contained: "We promise ... that [each episode] requires NO knowledge of the episode(s) that preceded it ... there is no 'Ultimate Mystery' which requires solving." While such statements contradicted their true plans, the ruse succeeded in persuading ABC to purchase the show.[[53]](#cite_note-lussier20130920-54) The game *[Myst](/wiki/Myst "Myst")*, also set in a tropical island, was noted as an influence by Lindelof, as in its narrative, "No one told you what the rules were. You just had to walk around and explore these environments and gradually a story was told."[[54]](#cite_note-55)

Abrams created the sound opening of the show and its title card, which were inspired by *[The Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone_(1959_TV_series) "The Twilight Zone (1959 TV series)")*.[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57) He withdrew from production of *Lost* partway through the first season to direct *[Mission: Impossible III](/wiki/Mission:_Impossible_III "Mission: Impossible III")*,[[57]](#cite_note-58) leaving Lindelof and new executive producer Carlton Cuse to develop much of the overall [mythology of the series](/wiki/Mythology_of_Lost "Mythology of Lost") themselves.[[58]](#cite_note-59) However, Abrams briefly returned to help co-write the third-season premiere along with Lindelof. The development of the show was constrained by tight deadlines, as it had been commissioned late in the 2004 season's development cycle. Despite the short schedule, the creative team remained flexible enough to modify or create characters to fit actors they wished to cast.[[59]](#cite_note-season1dvd-60)

*Lost*'s two-part pilot episode was the most expensive in the network's history, reportedly costing between US$10 and $14 million,[[60]](#cite_note-61) compared to the average cost of an hour-long pilot in 2005 of $4 million.[[61]](#cite_note-EIDC050405-62) The world premiere of the pilot episode was on July 24, 2004, at [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con").[[62]](#cite_note-63) ABC's parent company [Disney](/wiki/Disney "Disney") fired Braun before *Lost*'s broadcast debut, partly because of low ratings at the network and also because he had [greenlighted](/wiki/Greenlight "Greenlight") such an expensive and risky project.[[48]](#cite_note-DT081405-49) The series debuted on September 22, 2004, becoming one of the biggest critical and commercial successes of the [2004 television season](/wiki/2004_in_television "2004 in television"). Along with fellow new series *[Desperate Housewives](/wiki/Desperate_Housewives "Desperate Housewives")* and *[Grey's Anatomy](/wiki/Grey%27s_Anatomy "Grey's Anatomy")*, *Lost* helped to reverse the flagging fortunes of ABC,[[63]](#cite_note-64) and its great success likely caused the network to ignore that the show almost immediately broke Lindelof and Abrams' promises to it regarding *Lost*'s plots.[[53]](#cite_note-lussier20130920-54)

### Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Casting")]

Many of the first season roles were a result of the executive producers' liking of various actors. The main character Jack was going to die in the pilot, and the role was planned for [Michael Keaton](/wiki/Michael_Keaton "Michael Keaton"). However, ABC executives were adamant that Jack live.[[64]](#cite_note-DVD-65) Before it was decided that Jack would live, Kate was to emerge as the leader of the survivors; she was conceived as a middle-aged businesswoman whose husband had apparently died in the crash, a role later fulfilled by the recurring character Rose. Dominic Monaghan auditioned for the role of Sawyer, who at the time was supposed to be a slick suit-wearing city con man. The producers enjoyed Monaghan's performance and changed the character of Charlie, a washed-up former rock star, to fit him. Jorge Garcia also auditioned for Sawyer, and the part of Hurley was written for him. When Josh Holloway auditioned for Sawyer, the producers liked the edge he brought to the character (he reportedly kicked a chair when he forgot his lines and got angry in the audition) and his [southern accent](/wiki/Southern_American_English "Southern American English"), so they changed Sawyer to fit Holloway's acting. Yunjin Kim auditioned for Kate, but the producers wrote the character of Sun for her and the character of Jin, portrayed by Daniel Dae Kim, to be her husband. Sayid, played by Naveen Andrews, was also not in the original script. Locke and Michael were written with their actors in mind. Emilie de Ravin, who played Claire, was cast in what was supposed to be a recurring role.[[64]](#cite_note-DVD-65) In the second season, Michael Emerson was contracted to play Ben ("Henry Gale") for three episodes. His role was extended to eight episodes because of his acting skills and eventually, for the whole of season three and later seasons.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Filming")]

[![Jack Bender sitting at a microphone.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Jack_Bender_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-Jack_Bender_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jack_Bender_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

Jack Bender directed the most episodes of the series and also served as an executive producer.

*Lost* was filmed on [Panavision](/wiki/Panavision "Panavision") [35 mm](/wiki/35mm_format "35mm format") cameras almost entirely on the Hawaiian island of [Oahu](/wiki/Oahu "Oahu"), given the easily accessible, wide diversity of filming locations. The original island scenes for the pilot were filmed at [Mokulē'ia](/wiki/Mokul%C4%93%27ia,_Hawai%27i "Mokulē'ia, Hawai'i") Beach, near the northwest tip of the island. Later beach scenes take place in secluded spots of the famous [North Shore](/wiki/North_Shore_(Oahu) "North Shore (Oahu)"). Cave scenes in the first season were filmed on a [sound stage](/wiki/Sound_stage "Sound stage") built at a [Xerox](/wiki/Xerox "Xerox") parts warehouse, which had been empty since an [employee mass shooting](/wiki/1999_Honolulu_shootings "1999 Honolulu shootings") took place there in 1999.[[66]](#cite_note-67) In 2006, the sound-stage and production offices moved to the Hawaii Film Office-operated [Hawaii Film Studio](/wiki/Hawaii_Film_Studio "Hawaii Film Studio"),[[67]](#cite_note-68) where the sets depicting Season 2's "Swan Station" and Season 3's "Hydra Station" interiors were built.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

Various urban areas in and around [Honolulu](/wiki/Honolulu "Honolulu") are used as stand-ins for locations around the world, including California, New York, [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"), [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"), South Korea, [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq"), Nigeria, United Kingdom, Paris, [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), Berlin, Maldives, and Australia. For example, scenes set in a [Sydney Airport](/wiki/Sydney_Airport "Sydney Airport") were filmed at the [Hawaii Convention Center](/wiki/Hawaii_Convention_Center "Hawaii Convention Center"), while a World War II-era bunker was used as both an [Iraqi Republican Guard](/wiki/Iraqi_Republican_Guard "Iraqi Republican Guard") installation and a [Dharma Initiative](/wiki/Dharma_Initiative "Dharma Initiative") research station. Scenes set in Germany during the winter were filmed on Merchant St., with crushed ice scattered everywhere to create snow and Russian store and automobile signs on the street. Several scenes in the Season 3 finale, "[Through the Looking Glass](/wiki/Through_the_Looking_Glass_(Lost) "Through the Looking Glass (Lost)")", were shot in Los Angeles, including a hospital set borrowed from *Grey's Anatomy*. Two scenes during season four were filmed in London, because [Alan Dale](/wiki/Alan_Dale "Alan Dale"), who portrays Widmore, was at the time performing in the musical *[Spamalot](/wiki/Spamalot "Spamalot")* and was unable to travel to Hawaii.[[69]](#cite_note-70) Extensive archives of filming locations are tracked at a repository at the *Lost* Virtual Tour.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

### Promotion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Promotion")]

During its six years of broadcasting, *Lost* developed an extensive collection of promotional tools, ranging from the traditional promotions of the TV show made by the channel to the creation of [alternate reality games](/wiki/Alternate_reality_games "Alternate reality games"), such as the *[Lost Experience](/wiki/Lost_Experience "Lost Experience")*.[[71]](#cite_note-promotion-72) *Lost* showed innovation in the use of new advertising strategies in the sector and the transformation of the conventional devices used previously.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [*Lost Original Television Soundtracks*](/wiki/Lost_Original_Television_Soundtracks "Lost Original Television Soundtracks")

*Lost* features an [orchestral score](/wiki/Incidental_music "Incidental music") performed by the [Hollywood Studio Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Hollywood_Studio_Symphony_Orchestra "Hollywood Studio Symphony Orchestra") and composed by [Michael Giacchino](/wiki/Michael_Giacchino "Michael Giacchino"), incorporating many recurring themes for subjects, such as events, locations, and characters. Giacchino achieved some of the sounds for the score using unusual instruments, such as striking suspended pieces of the plane's [fuselage](/wiki/Fuselage "Fuselage").[[72]](#cite_note-73) On March 21, 2006, the record label [Varèse Sarabande](/wiki/Var%C3%A8se_Sarabande "Varèse Sarabande") released the original television soundtrack for *Lost*'s first season.[[73]](#cite_note-Soundtrack_1-74) The soundtrack included select full-length versions of the most popular themes of the season and the main title, which was composed by series creator J. J. Abrams.[[73]](#cite_note-Soundtrack_1-74) Varèse Sarabande released a soundtrack featuring music from season 2 of *Lost* on October 3, 2006.[[74]](#cite_note-Soundtrack_2-75) The soundtrack for season 3 was released on May 6, 2008; the soundtrack for season 4 was released on May 11, 2009; the soundtrack for season 5 was released on May 11, 2010; and the soundtrack for the final season was released on September 14, 2010. A final soundtrack, featuring music from series finale, was released on October 11, 2010.

The series uses pop culture songs sparingly, and has a mainly orchestral score (consisting usually of divided strings, percussion, harp, and three trombones). When it features pop songs, they often originate from a [diegetic](/wiki/Source_cue "Source cue") source. Examples include the various songs played on Hurley's portable CD player throughout the first season (until its batteries died in the episode "[...In Translation](/wiki/...In_Translation "...In Translation")", which featured Damien Rice's "Delicate") or the use of the record player in the second season, which included [Cass Elliot](/wiki/Cass_Elliot "Cass Elliot")'s "[Make Your Own Kind of Music](/wiki/Make_Your_Own_Kind_of_Music_(song) "Make Your Own Kind of Music (song)")" and [Petula Clark](/wiki/Petula_Clark "Petula Clark")'s "[Downtown](/wiki/Downtown_(Petula_Clark_song) "Downtown (Petula Clark song)")" in the second and third-season premieres respectively. Two episodes show Charlie on a street corner playing guitar and singing the [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)") song "[Wonderwall](/wiki/Wonderwall_(song) "Wonderwall (song)")". In the third season's finale, Jack drives down the street listening to [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)")'s "Scentless Apprentice", right before he arrives to the Hoffs/Drawlar Funeral Parlor, and in the parallel scene in the fourth season's finale, he arrives listening to "Gouge Away" by [Pixies](/wiki/Pixies_(band) "Pixies (band)"). The third season also used [Three Dog Night](/wiki/Three_Dog_Night "Three Dog Night")'s "[Shambala](/wiki/Shambala_(song) "Shambala (song)")" on two occasions in the van. The only two pop songs that have ever been used without an on-screen source (i.e., non-[diegetic](/wiki/Diegetic "Diegetic")) are [Ann-Margret](/wiki/Ann-Margret "Ann-Margret")'s "Slowly", in the episode "[I Do](/wiki/I_Do_(Lost) "I Do (Lost)")" and "I Shall Not Walk Alone", written by [Ben Harper](/wiki/Ben_Harper "Ben Harper") and covered by [The Blind Boys of Alabama](/wiki/The_Blind_Boys_of_Alabama "The Blind Boys of Alabama") in the episode "[Confidence Man](/wiki/Confidence_Man_(Lost) "Confidence Man (Lost)")". Alternate music is used in several international broadcasts. For example, in the Japanese broadcast of *Lost*, the [theme song](/wiki/Theme_music "Theme music") used varies by season: season one uses "Here I Am" by [Chemistry](/wiki/Chemistry_(band) "Chemistry (band)"), season two uses "[Losin'](/wiki/Losin%27 "Losin'")" by [Yuna Ito](/wiki/Yuna_Ito "Yuna Ito"), and season three uses "Lonely Girl" by [Crystal Kay](/wiki/Crystal_Kay "Crystal Kay").

### Allegations of toxic work environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Allegations of toxic work environment")]

In May 2023, [Maureen Ryan](/wiki/Maureen_Ryan "Maureen Ryan")'s book *Burn It Down: Power, Complicity and a Call For Change in Hollywood* cited claims made by cast member [Harold Perrineau](/wiki/Harold_Perrineau "Harold Perrineau") and writers [Monica Owusu-Breen](/wiki/Monica_Owusu-Breen "Monica Owusu-Breen") and [Melinda Hsu Taylor](/wiki/Melinda_Hsu_Taylor "Melinda Hsu Taylor") of an alleged toxic and racist environment fostered by Lindelof and Cuse. Perrineau said he was fired by Lindelof after he asked about his character's death. In response to the allegations, Lindelof stated "my level of fundamental inexperience as a manager and a boss, my role as someone who was supposed to model a climate of creative danger and risk-taking but provide safety and comfort inside of the creative process—I failed in that endeavor".[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77) Recurring cast member [Mira Furlan](/wiki/Mira_Furlan "Mira Furlan") also recalled an abusive on-set experience in her memoir. She regarded the entire cast as complicit, with the exception of one main actress.[[77]](#cite_note-memoir-78)

## Reception and impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Reception and impact")]

### Critical reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Critical reception")]

Critical response of *Lost*

| Season | [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") | [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic") |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | 96% (46 reviews)[[78]](#cite_note-79) | 86 (27 reviews)[[79]](#cite_note-80) |
| 2 | 100% (17 reviews)[[80]](#cite_note-81) | —N/a |
| 3 | 75% (20 reviews)[[81]](#cite_note-82) | —N/a |
| 4 | 89% (27 reviews)[[82]](#cite_note-83) | 87 (12 reviews)[[83]](#cite_note-MetacriticS4-84) |
| 5 | 92% (24 reviews)[[84]](#cite_note-85) | 78 (17 reviews)[[85]](#cite_note-MetacriticS5-86) |
| 6 | 69% (35 reviews)[[86]](#cite_note-87) | —N/a |

*Lost* has been described by numerous critics as being among the [greatest television series of all time](/wiki/List_of_television_shows_considered_the_best "List of television shows considered the best").[[87]](#cite_note-Time_100-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90) Bill Carter, television reporter of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, defined *Lost* as "the show with perhaps the most compelling continuing story line in television history."[[90]](#cite_note-91) *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* put the show on its end-of-the-decade, "best-of" list, saying, "Name another network drama that can so wondrously turn a ? into a !"[[91]](#cite_note-92) In 2012, *Entertainment Weekly* also listed the show at #10 in the "25 Best Cult TV Shows from the Past 25 Years", with a hot-and-cold description: "*Lost* was initially celebrated as a moving character-driven drama with a broad humanistic worldview that also presented itself as dramatic cryptography that demanded to be solved. The appeal narrowed as seasons progressed and the mythology became more complex, culminating in a still-debated finale that was deeply meaningful to some and dissatisfying poppycock to others."[[92]](#cite_note-93) In 2007, [TV Guide](/wiki/TV_Guide "TV Guide") ranked *Lost* as the #5 cult show.[[93]](#cite_note-94) In 2013, TV Guide ranked it as the #5 sci-fi show[[94]](#cite_note-95) and the #36 best series of all time.[[95]](#cite_note-96) In September 2019, *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* ranked the show 71st on its list of the 100 best TV shows of the 21st century.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

#### Season 1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Season 1")]

The first season received critical acclaim. *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")* said a "totally original, fabulously enjoyable lost-at-sea series", *Lost* had taken "an outlandish Saturday-serial setup and imbued it with real characters and honest emotions, without sacrificing any of the old-fashioned fun."[[97]](#cite_note-98) The *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* praised the production values and said "it knows the buttons it wants to push (fear of flying, fear of abandonment, fear of the unknown) and pushes them, repeatedly, like a kid playing a video game."[[98]](#cite_note-99) IGN noted that the first season "succeeded first and foremost in character development."[[99]](#cite_note-100) *Lost* season one was ranked number one in the "Best of 2005 TV Coverage: Critic Top Ten Lists" by Matthew Gilbert of *[The Boston Globe](/wiki/The_Boston_Globe "The Boston Globe")*, Tom Gliatto of *[People Weekly](/wiki/People_Weekly "People Weekly")*, Charlie McCollum of the *[San Jose Mercury News](/wiki/San_Jose_Mercury_News "San Jose Mercury News")*, and Robert Bianco of *USA Today*.[[100]](#cite_note-M1-101)

#### Season 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Season 2")]

The second season received favorable reviews, but it was noted that the season "stumbled with some storylines going nowhere and some characters underutilized." [IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN") also noted the addition of [Desmond Hume](/wiki/Desmond_Hume "Desmond Hume") as a standout new character.[[101]](#cite_note-102) The *[San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle")* called Season 2 an "extended, mostly unsatisfying foray into deeper mythology with very little payoff."[[102]](#cite_note-Goodman,_Tim-103) After winning "Best Drama Series" for season one, *Lost* was snubbed by the Emmy Awards in Season 2. Near the end of Lost's second season, *USA Today* listed the most popular fan theories during Season 2—the island as a psychological experiment, that the hatch had electromagnetic properties, [string theory](/wiki/String_theory "String theory") of time, and that everyone on the island had developed a "collective consciousness" that allowed them to appear in each other's past. One fan interview by *USA Today* said that "Real suspense comes from answers, not questions. Suspense comes not from wondering what's going on but from wondering what happens next. If you withhold answers, it becomes impossible to satisfy."[[103]](#cite_note-104)

#### Season 3

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Season 3")]

The first block of episodes of the third season was criticized for raising too many mysteries[[104]](#cite_note-105) and not providing enough answers.[[105]](#cite_note-106) Complaints were also made about the limited screen time for many of the main characters in the first block.[[106]](#cite_note-107) Locke, played by Terry O'Quinn, who had tied for the highest second-season episode count, appeared in only 13 of 23 episodes in the third season—only two more than guest star M.C. Gainey, who played Tom. Reaction to two new characters, Nikki and Paulo, was generally negative, and Lindelof even acknowledged that the couple was "universally despised" by fans.[[107]](#cite_note-108) The decision to split the season and the American time-slot switch after the hiatus were also criticized.[[108]](#cite_note-109)[[109]](#cite_note-110) Cuse acknowledged that, "No one was happy with the six-episode run."[[110]](#cite_note-111) The second block of episodes was critically acclaimed, however,[[111]](#cite_note-112) with the crew dealing with problems from the first block.[[112]](#cite_note-113) More answers were written into the show,[[113]](#cite_note-114) and Nikki and Paulo were [killed off](/wiki/Expos%C3%A9_(Lost) "Exposé (Lost)").[[114]](#cite_note-115) It was also announced that the series would end three seasons after the third season,[[115]](#cite_note-enddate-116) which Cuse hoped would tell the audience that the writers knew where the story was going.[[116]](#cite_note-117)

#### Season 4

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Season 4")]

[Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic") gave season four a weighted average of 87 based on 12 critical reviews,[[83]](#cite_note-MetacriticS4-84) For the first time since season one, *Lost* received an Emmy nomination for 'Outstanding Drama Series'. Tim Goodman of the *San Francisco Chronicle* said that the Season 4 episodes were "roller coasters of fast action and revelation" and that series was "back on track."[[102]](#cite_note-Goodman,_Tim-103) In a survey conducted by *TVWeek* of professional critics, *Lost* was voted the best show on television in the first half of 2008 "by a wide margin", apparently "crack[ing] the top five on nearly every critic's submission" and receiving "nothing but praise."[[117]](#cite_note-118) *The New York Times* said the show reveled in critiques of capitalism, using the fictional Mittelos Bioscience and the "malevolent British industrialist" character of Charles Widmore as examples. The critic also said that the show was "in the dark business of exploring just how futile the modern search for peace, knowledge, recovery or profit really is." The critic did go on to say that the series was not as "philosophically refined" as *[The Sopranos](/wiki/The_Sopranos "The Sopranos")* or *The Wire* but that it "has maximized the potential of narrative uncertainty and made it a beguiling constant."[[118]](#cite_note-119)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Michael_Emerson_and_Damon_Lindelof_at_the_68th_Annual_Peabody_Awards_for_Lost.jpg/250px-Michael_Emerson_and_Damon_Lindelof_at_the_68th_Annual_Peabody_Awards_for_Lost.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Emerson_and_Damon_Lindelof_at_the_68th_Annual_Peabody_Awards_for_Lost.jpg)

Michael Emerson and Damon Lindelof at the 68th Annual Peabody Awards for *Lost*

#### Season 5

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Season 5")]

The fifth season once again received mostly positive critical reception. Season 5 was given a weighted average of 78 out of 100 by Metacritic.[[85]](#cite_note-MetacriticS5-86) *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* said that "The ABC series remains one of primetime's most uncompromising efforts, and this year's latest wrinkle on flashbacks, flash-forwards and island-disappearing flashes of light does nothing to alter that perception."[[119]](#cite_note-120) [Alan Sepinwall](/wiki/Alan_Sepinwall "Alan Sepinwall") of *[The Star-Ledger](/wiki/The_Star-Ledger "The Star-Ledger")* said that season 5 may finally be "a day of reckoning between those viewers who embrace the show's science-fiction trappings, and those who prefer not to think about them." Sepinwall also related that "I loved every minute. But I'm also a geek who read [Ray Bradbury](/wiki/Ray_Bradbury "Ray Bradbury") and [Isaac Asimov](/wiki/Isaac_Asimov "Isaac Asimov") growing up."[[120]](#cite_note-121)

Heather Havrilesky of [Salon.com](/wiki/Salon.com "Salon.com") criticized the use of time travel, saying that "when a narrator brings magic or time travel or an act of God into the picture, then uses it without restraint, the story loses its anchor to real life." The critic also asked "Why does it matter what Locke and Richard Alpert and Daniel Faraday or anyone else does, when they all seem as clueless and unfettered from reality as we are as viewers? How can these characters have any concrete agenda or strategic approach or philosophical perspective on anything when the rug is pulled out from under them by another Act of God every few seconds?"[[121]](#cite_note-122)

*[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* also commented that "what has been most dispiriting about the current season is the show's willingness to abandon many of the larger and more compelling themes that grounded the elaborate plot: the struggles between faith and reason; the indictments of extreme capitalism, the futility of recovery. All that remains is the reductively limned battle between fate and free will largely playing out, now, in Jack Shephard's belief that returning to the island is his Destiny."[[122]](#cite_note-123) *[The A.V. Club](/wiki/The_A.V._Club "The A.V. Club")* said of the fifth-season finale, "Me? I found the ending frustrating, but in a good way. This finale was entertaining as all get-out to me, and despite the occasional groaner moment, I think this may be Lost's most purposeful, surprising finale."[[123]](#cite_note-124)

#### Season 6

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Season 6")]

Season six opened to much hype and curiosity. *The A.V. Club* asked, "I'm guessing that one of the biggest fears of *Lost* fans as we ride out this sixth and final season—bumps and all—is that we're going to come to the end and find a big nothing in return for all we've invested in these characters. We don't just need answers, we need justifications. Why has whatever happened, happened? Who has called this particular meeting to order, and does it really matter who showed up?"[[124]](#cite_note-125) The episodes "[The Substitute](/wiki/The_Substitute_(Lost) "The Substitute (Lost)")", "[Dr. Linus](/wiki/Dr._Linus "Dr. Linus")", "[Ab Aeterno](/wiki/Ab_Aeterno "Ab Aeterno")", "[Happily Ever After](/wiki/Happily_Ever_After_(Lost) "Happily Ever After (Lost)")", "[The Candidate](/wiki/The_Candidate_(Lost) "The Candidate (Lost)")", and "[What They Died For](/wiki/What_They_Died_For "What They Died For")" opened to highly positive critical reception while the third-to-last episode "[Across the Sea](/wiki/Across_the_Sea_(Lost) "Across the Sea (Lost)")" was the episode with the most polarized reception of the season.[[125]](#cite_note-Metacritic-126) The time spent at the Others' temple was criticized.[[126]](#cite_note-127) [E!](/wiki/E! "E!") Online described the show as "lightning in a bottle" and picked it as "Top TV Drama of 2010".[[127]](#cite_note-128)

#### Series finale

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Series finale")]

The series finale opened to highly polarized critical and fan reception. "The End" received "generally favorable reviews" with a Metacritic score of 74 out of 100 based on 31 critic reviews.[[125]](#cite_note-Metacritic-126) *[IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN")* reviewer Chris Carbot gave the finale a 10 out of 10, tying it with the initial review of "[Pilot, Part 1](/wiki/Pilot_(Lost) "Pilot (Lost)")", "[Through the Looking Glass](/wiki/Through_the_Looking_Glass_(Lost) "Through the Looking Glass (Lost)")", "[The Constant](/wiki/The_Constant "The Constant")", and "[There's No Place Like Home, Parts 2 & 3](/wiki/There%27s_No_Place_Like_Home_(Lost) "There's No Place Like Home (Lost)")" as the best-reviewed episode of *Lost*. He described it as "one of the most enthralling, entertaining and satisfying conclusions I could have hoped for." Carbot also noted that the discussions about the episode may never end, saying "*Lost* may be gone, but it will hardly be forgotten."[[128]](#cite_note-IGN-129) Eric Deggans of the *[St. Petersburg Times](/wiki/St._Petersburg_Times "St. Petersburg Times")* also gave the finale a perfect score, stating "Sunday's show was an emotional, funny, expertly measured reminder of what Lost has really centered on since its first moments on the prime time TV landscape: faith, hope, romance and the power of redemption through belief in the best of what moves mankind."[[129]](#cite_note-130) Robert Bianco of *[USA Today](/wiki/USA_Today "USA Today")* rated the episode perfect as well, deeming the finale "can stand with the best any series has produced."[[130]](#cite_note-131) Hal Boedeker of *[Orlando Sentinel](/wiki/Orlando_Sentinel "Orlando Sentinel")* cited the finale being "a stunner."[[131]](#cite_note-132)

*[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* and *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* both reported that "The End" had received negative reviews and disappointed its viewers. Alan Sepinwall of *[Star-Ledger](/wiki/Star-Ledger "Star-Ledger")* was less enthusiastic of the finale, stating "I'm still wrestling with my feelings about 'The End'... I thought most of it worked like gangbusters. ... But as someone who did spend at least part of the last six years dwelling on the questions that were unanswered—be they little things like the outrigger shootout or why The Others left Dharma in charge of the Swan station after the purge, or bigger ones like Walt—I can't say I found 'The End' wholly satisfying, either as closure for this season or the series. ... There are narrative dead ends in every season of 'Lost,' but it felt like season six had more than usual."[[132]](#cite_note-133) Mike Hale of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* gave "The End" a mixed review, as the episode showed that the series was "shaky on the big picture—on organizing the welter of mythic-religious-philosophical material it insisted on incorporating into its plot—but highly skilled at the small one, the moment to moment business of telling an exciting story. Rendered insignificant ... were the particulars of what they had done on the island."[[133]](#cite_note-134) [David Zurawik](/wiki/David_Zurawik "David Zurawik") of the *[Baltimore Sun](/wiki/Baltimore_Sun "Baltimore Sun")* gave the episode a highly negative review, writing "If this is supposed to be such a smart and wise show, unlike anything else on network TV (blah, blah, blah), why such a wimpy, phony, quasi-religious, white-light, huggy-bear ending. ... Once Jack stepped into the church it looked like he was walking into a Hollywood wrap party without food or music—just a bunch of actors grinning idiotically for 10 minutes and hugging one another."[[134]](#cite_note-135)

### Ratings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Ratings")]

*Lost* aired on the [American Broadcasting Company](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") (ABC) from September 22, 2004, to May 23, 2010. The pilot episode had 18.6 million viewers, easily winning its 9:00 pm timeslot, and giving ABC its strongest [ratings](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings") since 2000, when *[Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?](/wiki/Who_Wants_to_Be_a_Millionaire%3F "Who Wants to Be a Millionaire?")* was initially aired—beaten only the following month by the premiere of *[Desperate Housewives](/wiki/Desperate_Housewives "Desperate Housewives")*. According to *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")*, "ABC sure could use a breakout drama success, as it hasn't had a real hit since *[The Practice](/wiki/The_Practice "The Practice")*. *Lost* represents the network's best start for a drama with 18- to 49-year-olds since *[Once and Again](/wiki/Once_and_Again "Once and Again")* in 1999, and in total viewers since *[Murder One](/wiki/Murder_One_(TV_series) "Murder One (TV series)")* in 1995."[[135]](#cite_note-136)

For its first season, *Lost* averaged 16 million viewers, ranking 14th in viewership among prime-time shows and 15th among the eighteen to forty-nine-year-old [demographic](/wiki/Demographic "Demographic").[[136]](#cite_note-137) Its second season fared equally well: again, *Lost* ranked 14th in viewership, with an average of 15.5 million viewers. However, it improved its rating with 18- to 49-year-olds, ranking eighth.[[137]](#cite_note-138) The second-season premiere was even more viewed than the first, pulling in over 23 million viewers and setting a series record.[[138]](#cite_note-139) The third-season premiere brought in 18.8 million viewers. The seventh episode of the season, back from a three-month hiatus, saw a drop to 14.5 million. Over the course of the spring season, ratings would plunge to as low as 11 million viewers before recovering to near 14 million for the season finale. The ratings drop was partially explained when [Nielsen](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings") released [DVR](/wiki/Digital_video_recorder "Digital video recorder") ratings, showing *Lost* as the most recorded series on television. However, despite overall ratings losses, *Lost* still won its hour in the crucial 18–49 demographic and put out the highest 18–49 numbers in the 10:00 p.m. time slot ahead of any show on any network that season. The fourth-season premiere saw an increase from the previous episode to 16.1 million viewers,[[139]](#cite_note-season_4_ratings-140) though by the eighth episode, viewers had decreased to a series low of 11.461 million.[[139]](#cite_note-season_4_ratings-140) A survey of 20 countries by *Informa Telecoms and Media* in 2006 concluded that *Lost* was the second most popular TV show in those countries, after *[CSI: Miami](/wiki/CSI:_Miami "CSI: Miami")*.[[140]](#cite_note-141) The sixth-season premiere was the first to climb in the ratings year-over-year since the second season, drawing 12.1 million viewers.[[141]](#cite_note-The_Live_Feed-142)
