*Lost* gained renewed popularity after becoming available on streaming platforms like [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu") and [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix"). [Nielsen Media Research](/wiki/Nielsen_Media_Research "Nielsen Media Research"), which records streaming viewership on U.S. television screens, estimated that the series was watched for 678 million minutes from July 29 to August 4, 2024.[[142]](#cite_note-143)[[143]](#cite_note-144) It later accumulated 1.03 billion minutes of watch time from October 21–27, 2024.[[144]](#cite_note-145)

### Awards and nominations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Awards and nominations")]

Main article: [List of awards and nominations received by *Lost*](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Lost "List of awards and nominations received by Lost")

Capping its successful first season, *Lost* won the [Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series](/wiki/57th_Primetime_Emmy_Awards#Outstanding_Drama_Series "57th Primetime Emmy Awards")[[145]](#cite_note-146) and J. J. Abrams was awarded an Emmy in September 2005 for his work as the director of "Pilot". Terry O'Quinn and Naveen Andrews were nominated in the Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series category.[[8]](#cite_note-emmys-9) *Lost* swept the guild awards in 2005, winning the [Writers Guild of America Awards 2005](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America_Awards_2005 "Writers Guild of America Awards 2005") for Outstanding Achievement in Writing for a Dramatic Television Series,[[146]](#cite_note-WGA-147) the 2005 Producers Guild Award for Best Production,[[147]](#cite_note-148) the 2005 Director's Guild Award for Best Direction of a Dramatic Television Program,[[148]](#cite_note-dgaawards05-149) and the [2005 Screen Actors Guild Awards](/wiki/12th_Screen_Actors_Guild_Awards "12th Screen Actors Guild Awards") for Best Ensemble Cast.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Michael_Emerson_SDCC_2013.jpg/250px-Michael_Emerson_SDCC_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Emerson_SDCC_2013.jpg)

For his portrayal of [Ben Linus](/wiki/Ben_Linus "Ben Linus"), Michael Emerson received many awards and nominations, including winning a [Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Supporting_Actor_in_a_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series") in 2009.

It was nominated for a Golden Globe Award for Best Television Drama Series three times (2005–2007), and it won the award in 2006.[[149]](#cite_note-Globes-150) In 2006, Matthew Fox and Naveen Andrews received Golden Globe nominations for [Best Lead Actor in a Drama Series](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actor_%E2%80%93_Television_Series_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Actor – Television Series Drama")[[149]](#cite_note-Globes-150) and [Best Supporting Actor](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actor_%E2%80%93_Series,_Miniseries_or_Television_Film "Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actor – Series, Miniseries or Television Film")[[149]](#cite_note-Globes-150) respectively, and in 2007, Evangeline Lilly received a nomination for [Best Actress in a Television Drama Series](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actress_%E2%80%93_Television_Series_Drama "Golden Globe Award for Best Actress – Television Series Drama").[[149]](#cite_note-Globes-150) *Lost* was nominated for the 2005 [British Academy of Film and Television Award](/wiki/BAFTA_Award "BAFTA Award") for Best International.[[150]](#cite_note-151) In 2006, Jorge Garcia and Michelle Rodriguez took home [ALMA Awards](/wiki/ALMA_Awards "ALMA Awards") for Best Supporting Actor and Actress, respectively, in a Television Series.[[151]](#cite_note-ALMA06-152) It won the [Saturn Award](/wiki/Saturn_Award "Saturn Award") for Best Television Series in both 2005 and 2006.[[152]](#cite_note-Saturn-153) In 2005, Terry O'Quinn won a Saturn Award for Best Supporting Actor in a television series, and in 2006,[[152]](#cite_note-Saturn-153) Matthew Fox won for Best Lead Actor.[[152]](#cite_note-Saturn-153) *Lost* won consecutive Television Critics Association Awards for Outstanding Achievement in Drama, for both its first and second seasons.[[153]](#cite_note-TCA_Win-154) Consecutively as well, it won in 2005 and 2006 the [Visual Effects Society Award](/wiki/Visual_Effects_Society_Award "Visual Effects Society Award") for Outstanding Supporting Visual Effects in a Broadcast Program.[[154]](#cite_note-155)[[155]](#cite_note-156) Malcolm David Kelley won a [Young Artist Award](/wiki/Young_Artist_Award "Young Artist Award") for his performance as Walt in 2006.[[156]](#cite_note-YAA-157)

In 2005, *Lost* was voted *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")*'s Entertainer of the Year. The show won a 2005 Prism Award for Charlie's drug storyline in the episodes "Pilot", "House of the Rising Sun", and "The Moth".[[157]](#cite_note-Prism-158) In 2007, *Lost* was listed as one of *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine's "100 Best TV Shows of All-*TIME*".[[87]](#cite_note-Time_100-88) The series was nominated for but did not win a Writers Guild Award and Producers Guild Award again in 2007.[[158]](#cite_note-WGA07-159) In June 2007, *Lost* beat out over 20 nominated television shows from countries all over the globe to win the Best Drama award at the [Monte Carlo Television Festival](/wiki/Monte_Carlo_Television_Festival "Monte Carlo Television Festival"). In September 2007, both Michael Emerson and Terry O'Quinn were nominated for an [Emmy Award for Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series](/wiki/59th_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "59th Primetime Emmy Awards"), the award going to O'Quinn.[[159]](#cite_note-160) *Lost* was again nominated for Outstanding Drama Series at the [60th Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/60th_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "60th Primetime Emmy Awards") in 2008. The show also garnered seven other Emmy nominations, including Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series for Michael Emerson.[[8]](#cite_note-emmys-9) It won a [Peabody Award](/wiki/Peabody_Award "Peabody Award") in 2008.[[160]](#cite_note-161) In 2009, *Lost* was again nominated for Outstanding Drama Series, as well Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series for Michael Emerson at the [61st Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/61st_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "61st Primetime Emmy Awards"), of which the latter won.[[8]](#cite_note-emmys-9)

In 2010, the sixth and final season was nominated for twelve Emmy Awards at the [62nd Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/62nd_Primetime_Emmy_Awards "62nd Primetime Emmy Awards"), including Outstanding Drama Series; Outstanding Writing for a Drama Series, for Carlton Cuse and Damon Lindelof for the show's series finale, "The End"; Outstanding Lead Actor in a Drama Series, for Matthew Fox; Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series, for Michael Emerson and Terry O'Quinn; and Outstanding Guest Actress in a Drama Series, for Elizabeth Mitchell. It won only one Emmy (Outstanding Single-Camera Picture Editing) out of its twelve nominations for a series total of 11 wins and 55 nominations in its six-year run.[[161]](#cite_note-EMMY2010-162)

In 2010, [Kristin dos Santos](/wiki/Kristin_dos_Santos "Kristin dos Santos") of [E! Online](/wiki/E!_Online "E! Online") ranked *Lost* the best TV series of the past 20 years.[[162]](#cite_note-163) In 2013, the [Writers Guild of America](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America "Writers Guild of America") ranked *Lost* No. 27 in its list of the 101 Best Written TV Series of All Time.[[163]](#cite_note-164) In 2014, the series was nominated for the [TCA Heritage Award](/wiki/TCA_Heritage_Award "TCA Heritage Award").[[164]](#cite_note-165) In 2016, *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* ranked it the fourteenth best science fiction television show ever.[[165]](#cite_note-166)

### Fandom and popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Fandom and popular culture")]

As a mainstream [cult television](/wiki/Cult_television "Cult television") show, *Lost* has generated a dedicated and thriving international [fan](/wiki/Fan_(aficionado) "Fan (aficionado)") community. *Lost* fans, sometimes dubbed *Lostaways*[[166]](#cite_note-167) or *Losties*,[[167]](#cite_note-ABCpress20050512-168) have gathered at [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con") and conventions organized by ABC[[167]](#cite_note-ABCpress20050512-168)[[168]](#cite_note-169) but have also been active in developing many fan websites, including [Lostpedia](/wiki/Lostpedia "Lostpedia") and forums dedicated to the program and its related incarnations. Because of the show's elaborate mythology, its fansites have focused on speculation and theorizing about the island's mysteries, as well as on more typical fan activities, such as producing [fan fiction](/wiki/Fan_fiction "Fan fiction") and videos, compiling episode transcripts, [shipping](/wiki/Shipping_(fandom) "Shipping (fandom)") characters, and collecting memorabilia.[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171)[[171]](#cite_note-172)

Anticipating fan interest and trying to keep its audience engrossed, ABC embarked on various cross-media endeavors, often using new media. Fans of *Lost* have been able to explore ABC-produced tie-in websites, tie-in novels, an official forum sponsored by the creative team behind *Lost* ("The Fuselage"), "[mobisodes](/wiki/Mobisode "Mobisode")", podcasts by the producers, an official magazine, and an [alternate reality game](/wiki/Alternate_reality_game "Alternate reality game") (ARG) "[The Lost Experience](/wiki/The_Lost_Experience "The Lost Experience")."[[172]](#cite_note-173) An official fanclub was launched in the summer of 2005 through Creation Entertainment.[[167]](#cite_note-ABCpress20050512-168)

Due to the show's popularity, references to it and elements from its story have appeared in [parody](/wiki/Parody "Parody") and popular culture usage. These include appearances on television, such as on the series *[Fringe](/wiki/Fringe_(TV_series) "Fringe (TV series)")*, *[Will & Grace](/wiki/Will_%26_Grace "Will & Grace")*, *[Curb Your Enthusiasm](/wiki/Curb_Your_Enthusiasm "Curb Your Enthusiasm")*, *[30 Rock](/wiki/30_Rock "30 Rock")*,[[173]](#cite_note-174) *[Scrubs](/wiki/Scrubs_(TV_series) "Scrubs (TV series)")*, *[Modern Family](/wiki/Modern_Family "Modern Family")*, *[Orange Is the New Black](/wiki/Orange_Is_the_New_Black "Orange Is the New Black")*, *[Community](/wiki/Community_(TV_series) "Community (TV series)")*, *[The Office](/wiki/The_Office_(American_TV_series) "The Office (American TV series)")*, *[Family Guy](/wiki/Family_Guy "Family Guy")*, *[American Dad!](/wiki/American_Dad! "American Dad!")*, *[The Simpsons](/wiki/The_Simpsons "The Simpsons")*, *[Futurama](/wiki/Futurama "Futurama")*, *[Late Night with Jimmy Fallon](/wiki/Late_Night_with_Jimmy_Fallon "Late Night with Jimmy Fallon")* (under the sketch parody title "Late"), and *[The Venture Bros.](/wiki/The_Venture_Bros. "The Venture Bros.")*.[[174]](#cite_note-175) *Lost* is also featured as an [Easter egg](/wiki/Easter_egg_(media) "Easter egg (media)") in several video games, including *[Dead Island](/wiki/Dead_Island "Dead Island")*, *[Half-Life 2: Episode Two](/wiki/Half-Life_2:_Episode_Two "Half-Life 2: Episode Two")*, *[Fallout 3](/wiki/Fallout_3 "Fallout 3")*, *[World of Warcraft](/wiki/World_of_Warcraft "World of Warcraft")*, *[Just Cause 2](/wiki/Just_Cause_2 "Just Cause 2")*, *[Batman: Arkham City](/wiki/Batman:_Arkham_City "Batman: Arkham City")*, *[Grand Theft Auto V](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto_V "Grand Theft Auto V")*, and *[Singularity](/wiki/Singularity_(video_game) "Singularity (video game)")*.[[175]](#cite_note-176) Similarly, several songs have been published whose themes and titles were derived from the series, such as [Moneen](/wiki/Moneen "Moneen") ("Don't Ever Tell Locke What He Can't Do"), [Veil of Maya](/wiki/Veil_of_Maya "Veil of Maya") ("Namaste"), [Cosmo Jarvis](/wiki/Cosmo_Jarvis "Cosmo Jarvis") ("Lost"), [Senses Fail](/wiki/Senses_Fail "Senses Fail") ("Lost and Found" and "All the Best Cowboys Have Daddy Issues"), [Gatsbys American Dream](/wiki/Gatsbys_American_Dream "Gatsbys American Dream") ("You All Everybody" and "Station 5: The Pearl"), and [Punchline](/wiki/Punchline_(band) "Punchline (band)") ("Roller Coaster Smoke"). [Weezer](/wiki/Weezer "Weezer") named their eighth studio album [*Hurley*](/wiki/Hurley_(album) "Hurley (album)") after the character, with a photo of actor [Jorge Garcia](/wiki/Jorge_Garcia "Jorge Garcia") on the cover.[[176]](#cite_note-177)

After the episode "[Numbers](/wiki/Numbers_(Lost) "Numbers (Lost)")" aired on March 2, 2005, numerous people used the eponymous figures ([4, 8, 15, 16, 23, and 42](/wiki/4,_8,_15,_16,_23,_42 "4, 8, 15, 16, 23, 42")) as lottery entries. According to the *[Pittsburgh Tribune-Review](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Tribune-Review "Pittsburgh Tribune-Review")*, within three days, the numbers were tried over 500 times by local players.[[177]](#cite_note-178) By October 2005, thousands had tried them for the multi-state [Powerball](/wiki/Powerball "Powerball") lottery.[[178]](#cite_note-179) A study of the [Quebec Lottery](/wiki/Quebec_Lottery "Quebec Lottery") showed that the sequence was the third most popular choice of numbers for lottery players, behind only the [arithmetic sequences](/wiki/Arithmetic_sequence "Arithmetic sequence") 1–2–3–4–5–6 and 7–14–21–28–35–42.[[179]](#cite_note-180) The issue came to attention after a Mega Millions drawing for a near-record US$380,000,000 jackpot on January 4, 2011, drew a series of numbers in which the three lowest numbers (4–8–15) and the mega ball (42) matched four of the six numbers. The No. 42 is also the "Mega Number" in Hurley's "Mega Lotto" ticket. The players who played Hurley's exact combination won $150 each (or $118 in California).[[180]](#cite_note-181)

### Influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Influence")]

*Lost* has been cited as a key influence on several of its contemporaries.[[181]](#cite_note-182)[[182]](#cite_note-183) The [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") series *[FlashForward](/wiki/FlashForward "FlashForward")* was heavily compared to *Lost*, because of its similar use of [nonlinear narrative](/wiki/Nonlinear_narrative "Nonlinear narrative") and mysteries.[[183]](#cite_note-184)[[184]](#cite_note-185) The [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") series *[Heroes](/wiki/Heroes_(American_TV_series) "Heroes (American TV series)")* drew comparisons to *Lost* during its run, because of some similarities such as its [ensemble cast](/wiki/Ensemble_cast "Ensemble cast"). [Damon Lindelof](/wiki/Damon_Lindelof "Damon Lindelof") was involved in the early stages of the creative process of *Heroes*, as he was friends with *Heroes* creator [Tim Kring](/wiki/Tim_Kring "Tim Kring").[[185]](#cite_note-186) Ever since its premiere, *[The 100](/wiki/The_100_(TV_series) "The 100 (TV series)")* has been compared to *Lost* because of its similar setting and the importance of survival in its story.[[186]](#cite_note-187) The [TBS](/wiki/TBS_(American_TV_channel) "TBS (American TV channel)") comedy *[Wrecked](/wiki/Wrecked_(American_TV_series) "Wrecked (American TV series)")* has been defined as a [parody](/wiki/Parody "Parody") of *Lost*, because of its very similar premise and multiple references to the drama.[[187]](#cite_note-188)[[188]](#cite_note-189)

[Adam Horowitz](/wiki/Adam_Horowitz "Adam Horowitz") and [Edward Kitsis](/wiki/Edward_Kitsis "Edward Kitsis"), former writers of *Lost*, created the fantasy series *[Once Upon a Time](/wiki/Once_Upon_a_Time_(TV_series) "Once Upon a Time (TV series)")*, which has also been compared to *Lost*. Even though their series started after *Lost* ended, they conceived it in 2004.[[189]](#cite_note-190) Damon Lindelof was involved in the development of their series.[[190]](#cite_note-191) Despite the comparisons and similarities to *Lost*, the writers intended the shows to be very different from each other. To them, *Lost* concerned itself with redemption, while *Once Upon a Time* is about hope.[[191]](#cite_note-192) As a nod to the ties between the production teams of *Once Upon a Time* and *Lost*, the former show contains [allusions to *Lost*](/wiki/Mythology_of_Lost "Mythology of Lost"). For example, many items found in the *Lost* universe, such as [Apollo candy bars](/wiki/Apollo_Candy "Apollo Candy"), [Oceanic Airlines](/wiki/Oceanic_Airlines "Oceanic Airlines"), [Ajira Airways](/wiki/Ajira_Airways "Ajira Airways"), the TV series *Exposé* and MacCutcheon Whisky can be seen in *Once Upon a Time*.[[192]](#cite_note-Paleyfest2012-193)

Both during and after *Lost*'s run on ABC, its success led to dozens of new shows attempting to enter the same "mystery-driven sci-fi" genre as networks sought to cater to viewers' evolved affinity and demonstrated loyalty to this specific subset of drama.[[193]](#cite_note-194) In 2018, NBC introduced *[Manifest](/wiki/Manifest_(TV_series) "Manifest (TV series)")*, a show about the mysterious disappearance of a commercial passenger jet. While the show's preview and pilot alone sparked media coverage highlighting the show's central mystery to be seemingly the same as that of *Lost*, subsequent seasons have furthered controversy around the extent of key similarities. Noted parallels include the passengers' collective experience of [displacement in spacetime](/wiki/Time_travel "Time travel"), the acquisition of [supernatural abilities](/wiki/Supernatural_power "Supernatural power") and [medical phenomena](/wiki/Spontaneous_healing "Spontaneous healing") post-disappearance, the use of [flashback/flashforward](/wiki/Flashback_(narrative) "Flashback (narrative)") sequences to expose dark secrets harbored by passengers, the revelation of unusually high levels of [interconnectedness](/wiki/Degrees_of_separation "Degrees of separation") between the lives of passengers, the polarized beliefs held by passengers regarding [science versus faith](/wiki/Science_versus_religion "Science versus religion") as a means to explain their shared experience in different ways.[[194]](#cite_note-195) Season 4 of *Manifest* reveals a plot twist suggesting the passengers were in some way chosen, prompting critics to point out this same key plot twist introducing [predestination](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination") was featured in *Lost*.[[195]](#cite_note-196)

### *Getting Lost*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Getting Lost")]

Main article: [Getting Lost (film)](/wiki/Getting_Lost_(film) "Getting Lost (film)")

An [independent](/wiki/Independent_film "Independent film") [documentary film](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film") about the development and cultural impact of *Lost* titled *[Getting Lost](/wiki/Getting_Lost_(film) "Getting Lost (film)")* premiered on September 22, 2024, which marked the 20th anniversary of the original series' [premiere](/wiki/Pilot_(Lost) "Pilot (Lost)"). The film is directed by Taylor Morden and features interviews with the cast and crew of the show, along with members of the fan community. It also addresses the allegations of a toxic work environment that were revealed in 2023 during the film's production.[[196]](#cite_note-CB-197)

Canadian movie theater chain [Cineplex](/wiki/Cineplex_Entertainment "Cineplex Entertainment") held screenings of *Getting Lost* in theaters on November 3 and 6, 2024.[[197]](#cite_note-198)

## Distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Distribution")]

### Online

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Online")]

In addition to traditional terrestrial and [satellite television](/wiki/Satellite_television "Satellite television"), *Lost* is available from various online subscription services, including [Amazon Prime Video](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video"),[[198]](#cite_note-199) [Hulu](/wiki/Hulu "Hulu"),[[199]](#cite_note-200) on [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") outside of the US via [Star](/wiki/Star_(Disney%2B) "Star (Disney+)") and on [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix").[[200]](#cite_note-201) It was one of the first series issued through [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.")'s [iTunes Store](/wiki/ITunes_Store "ITunes Store") beginning in October 2005.[[201]](#cite_note-202) On August 29, 2007, *Lost* became one of the first TV programs available for download in the UK iTunes Store.[[202]](#cite_note-203)

In April 2006, [Disney](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company") announced that *Lost* would be available for free online in streaming format, with advertising, on ABC's website, as part of a two-month experiment of future distribution strategies. The trial, which ran from May to June 2006, caused a stir among network affiliates who were afraid of being cut out of advertising revenue. The streaming of *Lost* episodes direct from ABC's website was only available to viewers in the United States due to international licensing agreements.[[203]](#cite_note-204)[[204]](#cite_note-205) In 2009, *Lost* was named the most-watched show on the Internet based on viewers of episodes on ABC's website. The [Nielsen Company](/wiki/Nielsen_Company "Nielsen Company") reported that 1.425 million unique viewers have watched at least one episode on ABC's website.[[205]](#cite_note-206)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Home media")]

The first season of *Lost* was released under the title *[Lost: The Complete First Season](/wiki/Lost_season_1#DVD_release "Lost season 1")* as a [widescreen](/wiki/Widescreen "Widescreen") seven-disc [Region 1](/wiki/DVD_region_code#1 "DVD region code") DVD [box set](/wiki/Box_set "Box set") on September 6, 2005, two weeks before the premiere of the second season. It was distributed by Buena Vista Home Entertainment. In addition to all the episodes that had been aired, it included several DVD extras, such as episode commentaries, behind-the-scenes footage and making-of features as well as [deleted scenes](/wiki/Deleted_scenes "Deleted scenes"), deleted flashback scenarios, and a [blooper](/wiki/Blooper "Blooper") reel. The same set was released on November 30, 2005, in [Region 4](/wiki/DVD_region_code#4 "DVD region code").[[206]](#cite_note-207) The season was first released split into two parts: the first twelve episodes of season 1 were available as a widescreen four-disc Region 2 DVD box set on October 31, 2005, while the remaining thirteen episodes of season 1 were released on January 16, 2006.[[207]](#cite_note-208) The DVD features available on the Region 1 release were likewise split over the two box sets. The first two seasons were released separately on [Blu-ray Disc](/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc "Blu-ray Disc") on June 16, 2009.[[208]](#cite_note-209)

The second season was released under the title *[Lost: The Complete Second Season – The Extended Experience](/wiki/Lost_season_2#DVD_release "Lost season 2")* as a widescreen seven-disc Region 1 DVD box set on September 5, 2006. The sets include several DVD extras, including behind the scenes footage, deleted scenes, and a "*Lost* Connections" chart, which shows how all of the characters on the island are inter-connected.[[209]](#cite_note-210) Again, the season was initially delivered in two sets for Region 2: the first twelve episodes were released as a widescreen four-disc DVD box set on July 17, 2006, while the remaining episodes of season 2 were released as a four-disc DVD box set on October 2, 2006.[[210]](#cite_note-211) The set was released in Region 4 on October 4, 2006.

The third season was released under the title *[Lost: The Complete Third Season – The Unexplored Experience](/wiki/Lost_season_3#DVD_release "Lost season 3")* on DVD and Blu-ray in Region 1 on December 11, 2007.[[211]](#cite_note-3DVDBluRay-212) As with seasons 1 and 2, the third season release includes audio commentaries with the cast and crew, bonus featurettes, deleted scenes, and bloopers. The third season was released in Region 2 solely on DVD on October 22, 2007, though this time, only as a complete set, unlike previous seasons.[[212]](#cite_note-213)

The fourth season was released as *[Lost: The Complete Fourth Season – The Expanded Experience](/wiki/Lost_season_4#DVD_release "Lost season 4")* in Region 1 on December 9, 2008, on both DVD and Blu-ray Disc.[[213]](#cite_note-214) It was released on DVD in Region 2 on October 20, 2008.[[214]](#cite_note-215) The set includes audio commentaries, deleted scenes, bloopers, and bonus featurettes.

The first three seasons of *Lost* have sold successfully on DVD. The season 1 box set entered the DVD sales chart at number two in September 2005,[[215]](#cite_note-216) and the season 2 box set entered the DVD sales chart at the number one position in its first week of release in September 2006, believed to be the second TV-DVD ever to enter the chart at the top spot.[[216]](#cite_note-217) The season 3 box set sold over 1,000,000 copies in three weeks.[[217]](#cite_note-218)

Both the season 6 box set and the complete series collection contained a 12-minute epilogue-like bonus feature called "[The New Man in Charge](/wiki/The_New_Man_in_Charge "The New Man in Charge")".[[218]](#cite_note-219)[[219]](#cite_note-220) The season 6 DVD set entered the DVD sales chart at the number one position in its first week of release in September 2010 boasting strong sales in the DVD and Blu-ray format for the regular season set as well as for the series box set.[[220]](#cite_note-221)

## Other media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Other media")]

The characters and setting of *Lost* have appeared in several official tie-ins outside of the television broadcast, including in print, on the Internet, and in short videos for mobile phones. Three [novelizations](/wiki/Novelization "Novelization") have been released by [Hyperion Books](/wiki/Hachette_Books#Hyperion_Books "Hachette Books"), a publisher owned by [Disney](/wiki/Disney "Disney"), ABC's parent company. They are *Endangered Species* ([ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-7868-9090-8](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-7868-9090-8 "Special:BookSources/0-7868-9090-8")) and *Secret Identity* ([ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-7868-9091-6](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-7868-9091-6 "Special:BookSources/0-7868-9091-6")) both by Cathy Hapka and *Signs of Life* ([ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-7868-9092-4](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-7868-9092-4 "Special:BookSources/0-7868-9092-4")) by Frank Thompson. Additionally, Hyperion published a [metafictional](/wiki/Metafictional "Metafictional") book titled *Bad Twin* ([ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [1-4013-0276-9](/wiki/Special:BookSources/1-4013-0276-9 "Special:BookSources/1-4013-0276-9")), written by Laurence Shames,[[221]](#cite_note-222) and credited to fictitious author "[Gary Troup](/wiki/Gary_Troup_(Lost) "Gary Troup (Lost)")", who ABC's marketing department claimed was a passenger on Oceanic Flight 815.

Several unofficial books relating to the show have also been published. *Finding Lost: The Unofficial Guide* ([ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [1-55022-743-2](/wiki/Special:BookSources/1-55022-743-2 "Special:BookSources/1-55022-743-2")) by Nikki Stafford and published by [ECW Press](/wiki/ECW_Press "ECW Press") is a book detailing the show for fans and those new to the show. *What Can Be Found in Lost?* ([ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-7369-2121-4](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-7369-2121-4 "Special:BookSources/0-7369-2121-4")) by John Ankerberg and Dillon Burrough, published by Harvest House is the first book dedicated to an investigation of the spiritual themes of the series from a Christian perspective. *Living Lost: Why We're All Stuck on the Island* ([ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [1-891053-02-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/1-891053-02-7 "Special:BookSources/1-891053-02-7")) by J. Wood,[[222]](#cite_note-223) published by the Garett County Press, is the first work of cultural criticism based on the series. The book explores the show's strange engagement with the contemporary experiences of war, (mis)information, and terrorism and argues that the audience functions as a character in the narrative. The author also writes a blog column[[223]](#cite_note-224) during the second part of the third season for [Powell's Books](/wiki/Powell%27s_Books "Powell's Books"). Each post discusses the previous episode's literary, historical, philosophical, and narrative connections.

The show's networks and producers have made extensive use of the Internet in expanding the background of the story. For example, during the first season, a fictional diary by an unseen survivor called "Janelle Granger" was presented on the ABC web site for the series. Likewise, a tie-in website about the fictional [Oceanic Airlines](/wiki/Oceanic_Airlines "Oceanic Airlines") appeared during the first season, which included several [Easter eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg_(virtual) "Easter egg (virtual)") and clues about the show. Another tie-in website was launched after the airing of "[Orientation](/wiki/Orientation_(Lost) "Orientation (Lost)")" about the Hanso Foundation. In the UK, the interactive back-stories of several characters were included in "Lost Untold", a section of [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4")'s *Lost* website. Similarly, beginning in November 2005, ABC produced an official [podcast](/wiki/Podcast "Podcast"), hosted by series writers and executive producers Damon Lindelof and Carlton Cuse. The podcast typically features a discussion about the weekly episode, interviews with cast members, and questions from viewers.[[224]](#cite_note-225) [Sky1](/wiki/Sky1 "Sky1") also hosted a podcast presented by [Iain Lee](/wiki/Iain_Lee "Iain Lee") on their website, which analyzed each episode after it aired in the United Kingdom.[[225]](#cite_note-226)

The foray into the online realm culminated in the *[Lost Experience](/wiki/Lost_Experience "Lost Experience")*, an Internet-based alternate reality game produced by Channel 7 (Australia), ABC (America), and Channel Four (UK), which began in early May 2006. The game presents a five-phase parallel storyline, primarily involving the Hanso Foundation.[[226]](#cite_note-227)

Short mini-episodes ("[mobisodes](/wiki/Mobisode "Mobisode")") called the *Lost Video Diaries* were scheduled for viewing by [Verizon Wireless](/wiki/Verizon_Wireless "Verizon Wireless") subscribers via its V-Cast system but were delayed by contract disputes.[[227]](#cite_note-228)[[228]](#cite_note-229) The mobisodes were renamed *[Lost: Missing Pieces](/wiki/Lost:_Missing_Pieces "Lost: Missing Pieces")* and aired from November 7, 2007, to January 28, 2008.

### Licensed merchandise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Lost_(TV_series)&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Licensed merchandise")]

In addition to tie-in novels, several other products based on the series, such as toys and games, have been licensed for release. A video game, *[Lost: Via Domus](/wiki/Lost:_Via_Domus "Lost: Via Domus")*, was released to average reviews, developed by [Ubisoft](/wiki/Ubisoft "Ubisoft"), for [game consoles](/wiki/Game_consoles "Game consoles") and home computers,[[229]](#cite_note-230) while [Gameloft](/wiki/Gameloft "Gameloft") released Lost: The Mobile Game for mobile phones and [iPods](/wiki/IPod "IPod").[[230]](#cite_note-231) Cardinal Games released a *Lost* board game on August 7, 2006.[[231]](#cite_note-232) TDC Games created a series of four 1000-piece jigsaw puzzles ("The Hatch", "The Numbers", "The Others", and "Before the Crash"), which, when put together, reveal embedded clues to the overall mythology of *Lost*. Inkworks has published three sets of *Lost* [trading cards](/wiki/Trading_card "Trading card"), *Season One*, *Season Two*, and *Revelations*.[[232]](#cite_note-233) In May 2006, [McFarlane Toys](/wiki/McFarlane_Toys "McFarlane Toys") announced recurring lines of character [action figures](/wiki/Action_figures "Action figures")[[233]](#cite_note-234) and released the first series in November 2006, with the second series being released July 2007. Furthermore, ABC sold a myriad of *Lost* merchandise in their online store, including clothing, jewelry, and other collectibles.[[234]](#cite_note-235) In October 2010, [DK Publishing](/wiki/DK_Publishing "DK Publishing") released a 400-page reference titled *The Lost Encyclopedia*, written by Tara Bennett and Paul Terry. The book compiled information from the TV show producers "writers bible", listing nearly every character, chronological event, location, and plot detail of the series, filling in the gaps for die-hard fans.[[235]](#cite_note-236)
